title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## AN IMAGE IS WORTH 16X16 WORDS: TRANSFORMERS FOR IMAGE RECOGNITION AT SCALE

**Alexey Dosovitskiy** _[∗][,][†]_ **, Lucas Beyer** _[∗]_ **, Alexander Kolesnikov** _[∗]_ **, Dirk Weissenborn** _[∗]_ **, Xiaohua Zhai** _[∗]_ **, Thomas Unterthiner, Mostafa Dehghani, Matthias Minderer, Georg Heigold, Sylvain Gelly, Jakob Uszkoreit, Neil Houlsby** _[∗][,][†]_

> _∗_ equal technical contribution, _†_ equal advising Google Research, Brain Team

_{_ adosovitskiy, neilhoulsby _}_ @google.com

## ABSTRACT

While the Transformer architecture has become the de-facto standard for natural language processing tasks, its applications to computer vision remain limited. In vision, attention is either applied in conjunction with convolutional networks, or used to replace certain components of convolutional networks while keeping their overall structure in place. We show that this reliance on CNNs is not necessary and a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks. When pre-trained on large amounts of data and transferred to multiple mid-sized or small image recognition benchmarks (ImageNet, CIFAR-100, VTAB, etc.), Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train.[1]

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Self-attention-based architectures, in particular Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), have become the model of choice in natural language processing (NLP). The dominant approach is to pre-train on a large text corpus and then fine-tune on a smaller task-specific dataset (Devlin et al., 2019). Thanks to Transformers’ computational efficiency and scalability, it has become possible to train models of unprecedented size, with over 100B parameters (Brown et al., 2020; Lepikhin et al., 2020). With the models and datasets growing, there is still no sign of saturating performance.

In computer vision, however, convolutional architectures remain dominant (LeCun et al., 1989; Krizhevsky et al., 2012; He et al., 2016). Inspired by NLP successes, multiple works try combining CNN-like architectures with self-attention (Wang et al., 2018; Carion et al., 2020), some replacing the convolutions entirely (Ramachandran et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020a). The latter models, while theoretically efficient, have not yet been scaled effectively on modern hardware accelerators due to the use of specialized attention patterns. Therefore, in large-scale image recognition, classic ResNetlike architectures are still state of the art (Mahajan et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2020; Kolesnikov et al., 2020).

Inspired by the Transformer scaling successes in NLP, we experiment with applying a standard Transformer directly to images, with the fewest possible modifications. To do so, we split an image into patches and provide the sequence of linear embeddings of these patches as an input to a Transformer. Image patches are treated the same way as tokens (words) in an NLP application. We train the model on image classification in supervised fashion.

When trained on mid-sized datasets such as ImageNet without strong regularization, these models yield modest accuracies of a few percentage points below ResNets of comparable size. This seemingly discouraging outcome may be expected: Transformers lack some of the inductive biases

inherent to CNNs, such as translation equivariance and locality, and therefore do not generalize well

However, the picture changes if the models are trained on larger datasets (14M-300M images). We find that large scale training trumps inductive bias. Our Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results when pre-trained at sufficient scale and transferred to tasks with fewer datapoints. When pre-trained on the public ImageNet-21k dataset or the in-house JFT-300M dataset, ViT approaches or beats state of the art on multiple image recognition benchmarks. In particular, the best model reaches the accuracy of 88 _._ 55% on ImageNet, 90 _._ 72% on ImageNet-ReaL, 94 _._ 55% on CIFAR-100, and 77 _._ 63% on the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Transformers were proposed by Vaswani et al. (2017) for machine translation, and have since become the state of the art method in many NLP tasks. Large Transformer-based models are often pre-trained on large corpora and then fine-tuned for the task at hand: BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) uses a denoising self-supervised pre-training task, while the GPT line of work uses language modeling as its pre-training task (Radford et al., 2018; 2019; Brown et al., 2020).

Naive application of self-attention to images would require that each pixel attends to every other pixel. With quadratic cost in the number of pixels, this does not scale to realistic input sizes. Thus, to apply Transformers in the context of image processing, several approximations have been tried in the past. Parmar et al. (2018) applied the self-attention only in local neighborhoods for each query pixel instead of globally. Such local multi-head dot-product self attention blocks can completely replace convolutions (Hu et al., 2019; Ramachandran et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). In a different line of work, Sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019) employ scalable approximations to global selfattention in order to be applicable to images. An alternative way to scale attention is to apply it in blocks of varying sizes (Weissenborn et al., 2019), in the extreme case only along individual axes (Ho et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020a). Many of these specialized attention architectures demonstrate promising results on computer vision tasks, but require complex engineering to be implemented efficiently on hardware accelerators.

Most related to ours is the model of Cordonnier et al. (2020), which extracts patches of size 2 _×_ 2 from the input image and applies full self-attention on top. This model is very similar to ViT, but our work goes further to demonstrate that large scale pre-training makes vanilla transformers competitive with (or even better than) state-of-the-art CNNs. Moreover, Cordonnier et al. (2020) use a small patch size of 2 _×_ 2 pixels, which makes the model applicable only to small-resolution images, while we handle medium-resolution images as well.

There has also been a lot of interest in combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with forms of self-attention, e.g. by augmenting feature maps for image classification (Bello et al., 2019) or by further processing the output of a CNN using self-attention, e.g. for object detection (Hu et al., 2018; Carion et al., 2020), video processing (Wang et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2019), image classification (Wu et al., 2020), unsupervised object discovery (Locatello et al., 2020), or unified text-vision tasks (Chen et al., 2020c; Lu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).

Another recent related model is image GPT (iGPT) (Chen et al., 2020a), which applies Transformers to image pixels after reducing image resolution and color space. The model is trained in an unsupervised fashion as a generative model, and the resulting representation can then be fine-tuned or probed linearly for classification performance, achieving a maximal accuracy of 72% on ImageNet.

Our work adds to the increasing collection of papers that explore image recognition at larger scales than the standard ImageNet dataset. The use of additional data sources allows to achieve state-ofthe-art results on standard benchmarks (Mahajan et al., 2018; Touvron et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2020). Moreover, Sun et al. (2017) study how CNN performance scales with dataset size, and Kolesnikov et al. (2020); Djolonga et al. (2020) perform an empirical exploration of CNN transfer learning from large scale datasets such as ImageNet-21k and JFT-300M. We focus on these two latter datasets as well, but train Transformers instead of ResNet-based models used in prior works.

## 3 METHOD

In model design we follow the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) as closely as possible. An advantage of this intentionally simple setup is that scalable NLP Transformer architectures – and their efficient implementations – can be used almost out of the box.

## 3.1 VISION TRANSFORMER (VIT)

An overview of the model is depicted in Figure 1. The standard Transformer receives as input a 1D sequence of token embeddings. To handle 2D images, we reshape the image **x** _∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into a sequence of flattened 2D patches **x** _p ∈_ R _[N][×]_[(] _[P]_[ 2] _[·][C]_[)] , where ( _H, W_ ) is the resolution of the original image, _C_ is the number of channels, ( _P, P_ ) is the resolution of each image patch, and _N_ = _HW/P_[2] is the resulting number of patches, which also serves as the effective input sequence length for the Transformer. The Transformer uses constant latent vector size _D_ through all of its layers, so we flatten the patches and map to _D_ dimensions with a trainable linear projection (Eq. 1). We refer to the output of this projection as the patch embeddings.

Similar to BERT’s [class] token, we prepend a learnable embedding to the sequence of embedded patches ( **z**[0] 0[=] **[x]**[class][),][whose][state at the output of the Transformer encoder (] **[z]**[0] _L_[) serves as the] image representation **y** (Eq. 4). Both during pre-training and fine-tuning, a classification head is attached to **z**[0] _L_[. The classification head is implemented by a MLP with one hidden layer at pre-training] time and by a single linear layer at fine-tuning time.

Position embeddings are added to the patch embeddings to retain positional information. We use standard learnable 1D position embeddings, since we have not observed significant performance gains from using more advanced 2D-aware position embeddings (Appendix D.3). The resulting sequence of embedding vectors serves as input to the encoder.

The Transformer encoder (Vaswani et al., 2017) consists of alternating layers of multiheaded selfattention (MSA, see Appendix A) and MLP blocks (Eq. 2, 3). Layernorm (LN) is applied before every block, and residual connections after every block (Wang et al., 2019; Baevski & Auli, 2019).

The MLP contains two layers with a GELU non-linearity.

**Inductive bias.** We note that Vision Transformer has much less image-specific inductive bias than CNNs. In CNNs, locality, two-dimensional neighborhood structure, and translation equivariance are baked into each layer throughout the whole model. In ViT, only MLP layers are local and translationally equivariant, while the self-attention layers are global. The two-dimensional neighborhood structure is used very sparingly: in the beginning of the model by cutting the image into patches and at fine-tuning time for adjusting the position embeddings for images of different resolution (as described below). Other than that, the position embeddings at initialization time carry no information about the 2D positions of the patches and all spatial relations between the patches have to be learned from scratch.

**Hybrid Architecture.** As an alternative to raw image patches, the input sequence can be formed from feature maps of a CNN (LeCun et al., 1989). In this hybrid model, the patch embedding projection **E** (Eq. 1) is applied to patches extracted from a CNN feature map. As a special case, the patches can have spatial size 1x1, which means that the input sequence is obtained by simply flattening the spatial dimensions of the feature map and projecting to the Transformer dimension. The classification input embedding and position embeddings are added as described above.

## 3.2 FINE-TUNING AND HIGHER RESOLUTION

Typically, we pre-train ViT on large datasets, and fine-tune to (smaller) downstream tasks. For this, we remove the pre-trained prediction head and attach a zero-initialized _D × K_ feedforward layer, where _K_ is the number of downstream classes. It is often beneficial to fine-tune at higher resolution than pre-training (Touvron et al., 2019; Kolesnikov et al., 2020). When feeding images of higher resolution, we keep the patch size the same, which results in a larger effective sequence length. The Vision Transformer can handle arbitrary sequence lengths (up to memory constraints), however, the pre-trained position embeddings may no longer be meaningful. We therefore perform 2D interpolation of the pre-trained position embeddings, according to their location in the original image. Note that this resolution adjustment and patch extraction are the only points at which an inductive bias about the 2D structure of the images is manually injected into the Vision Transformer.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the representation learning capabilities of ResNet, Vision Transformer (ViT), and the hybrid. To understand the data requirements of each model, we pre-train on datasets of varying size and evaluate many benchmark tasks. When considering the computational cost of pre-training the model, ViT performs very favourably, attaining state of the art on most recognition benchmarks at a lower pre-training cost. Lastly, we perform a small experiment using self-supervision, and show that self-supervised ViT holds promise for the future.

## 4.1 SETUP

**Datasets.** To explore model scalability, we use the ILSVRC-2012 ImageNet dataset with 1k classes and 1.3M images (we refer to it as ImageNet in what follows), its superset ImageNet-21k with 21k classes and 14M images (Deng et al., 2009), and JFT (Sun et al., 2017) with 18k classes and 303M high-resolution images. We de-duplicate the pre-training datasets w.r.t. the test sets of the downstream tasks following Kolesnikov et al. (2020). We transfer the models trained on these dataset to several benchmark tasks: ImageNet on the original validation labels and the cleaned-up ReaL labels (Beyer et al., 2020), CIFAR-10/100 (Krizhevsky, 2009), Oxford-IIIT Pets (Parkhi et al., 2012), and Oxford Flowers-102 (Nilsback & Zisserman, 2008). For these datasets, pre-processing follows Kolesnikov et al. (2020).

Table 1: Details of Vision Transformer model variants.

We also evaluate on the 19-task VTAB classification suite (Zhai et al., 2019b). VTAB evaluates low-data transfer to diverse tasks, using 1 000 training examples per task. The tasks are divided into three groups: _Natural_ – tasks like the above, Pets, CIFAR, etc. _Specialized_ – medical and satellite imagery, and _Structured_ – tasks that require geometric understanding like localization.

**Model Variants.** We base ViT configurations on those used for BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), as summarized in Table 1. The “Base” and “Large” models are directly adopted from BERT and we add the larger “Huge” model. In what follows we use brief notation to indicate the model size and the input patch size: for instance, ViT-L/16 means the “Large” variant with 16 _×_ 16 input patch size. Note that the Transformer’s sequence length is inversely proportional to the square of the patch size, thus models with smaller patch size are computationally more expensive.

For the baseline CNNs, we use ResNet (He et al., 2016), but replace the Batch Normalization layers (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) with Group Normalization (Wu & He, 2018), and used standardized convolutions (Qiao et al., 2019). These modifications improve transfer (Kolesnikov et al., 2020), and we denote the modified model “ResNet (BiT)”. For the hybrids, we feed the intermediate feature maps into ViT with patch size of one “pixel”. To experiment with different sequence lengths, we either (i) take the output of stage 4 of a regular ResNet50 or (ii) remove stage 4, place the same number of layers in stage 3 (keeping the total number of layers), and take the output of this extended stage 3. Option (ii) results in a 4x longer sequence length, and a more expensive ViT model.

**Training & Fine-tuning.** We train all models, including ResNets, using Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) with _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9, _β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999, a batch size of 4096 and apply a high weight decay of 0 _._ 1, which we found to be useful for transfer of all models (Appendix D.1 shows that, in contrast to common practices, Adam works slightly better than SGD for ResNets in our setting). We use a linear learning rate warmup and decay, see Appendix B.1 for details. For fine-tuning we use SGD with momentum, batch size 512, for all models, see Appendix B.1.1. For ImageNet results in Table 2, we fine-tuned at higher resolution: 512 for ViT-L/16 and 518 for ViT-H/14, and also used Polyak & Juditsky (1992) averaging with a factor of 0 _._ 9999 (Ramachandran et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020b).

**Metrics.** We report results on downstream datasets either through few-shot or fine-tuning accuracy. Fine-tuning accuracies capture the performance of each model after fine-tuning it on the respective dataset. Few-shot accuracies are obtained by solving a regularized least-squares regression problem that maps the (frozen) representation of a subset of training images to _{−_ 1 _,_ 1 _}[K]_ target vectors. This formulation allows us to recover the exact solution in closed form. Though we mainly focus on fine-tuning performance, we sometimes use linear few-shot accuracies for fast on-the-fly evaluation where fine-tuning would be too costly.

## 4.2 COMPARISON TO STATE OF THE ART

We first compare our largest models – ViT-H/14 and ViT-L/16 – to state-of-the-art CNNs from the literature. The first comparison point is Big Transfer (BiT) (Kolesnikov et al., 2020), which performs supervised transfer learning with large ResNets. The second is Noisy Student (Xie et al., 2020), which is a large EfficientNet trained using semi-supervised learning on ImageNet and JFT300M with the labels removed. Currently, Noisy Student is the state of the art on ImageNet and BiT-L on the other datasets reported here. All models were trained on TPUv3 hardware, and we report the number of TPUv3-core-days taken to pre-train each of them, that is, the number of TPU v3 cores (2 per chip) used for training multiplied by the training time in days.

Table 2 shows the results. The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train. The larger model, ViT-H/14, further improves the performance, especially on the more challenging datasets – ImageNet, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite. Interestingly, this

ViT-H/14 outperforms BiT-R152x4, and other methods, on the _Natural_ and _Structured_ tasks. On the _Specialized_ the performance of the top two models is similar.

## 4.3 PRE-TRAINING DATA REQUIREMENTS

The Vision Transformer performs well when pre-trained on a large JFT-300M dataset. With fewer inductive biases for vision than ResNets, how crucial is the dataset size? We perform two series of experiments.

First, we pre-train ViT models on datasets of increasing size: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT300M. To boost the performance on the smaller datasets, we optimize three basic regularization parameters – weight decay, dropout, and label smoothing. Figure 3 shows the results after finetuning to ImageNet (results on other datasets are shown in Table 5)[2] . When pre-trained on the smallest dataset, ImageNet, ViT-Large models underperform compared to ViT-Base models, despite (moderate) regularization. With ImageNet-21k pre-training, their performances are similar. Only with JFT-300M, do we see the full benefit of larger models. Figure 3 also shows the performance

Second, we train our models on random subsets of 9M, 30M, and 90M as well as the full JFT300M dataset. We do not perform additional regularization on the smaller subsets and use the same hyper-parameters for all settings. This way, we assess the intrinsic model properties, and not the effect of regularization. We do, however, use early-stopping, and report the best validation accuracy achieved during training. To save compute, we report few-shot linear accuracy instead of full finetuning accuracy. Figure 4 contains the results. Vision Transformers overfit more than ResNets with comparable computational cost on smaller datasets. For example, ViT-B/32 is slightly faster than ResNet50; it performs much worse on the 9M subset, but better on 90M+ subsets. The same is true for ResNet152x2 and ViT-L/16. This result reinforces the intuition that the convolutional inductive bias is useful for smaller datasets, but for larger ones, learning the relevant patterns directly from data is sufficient, even beneficial.

Overall, the few-shot results on ImageNet (Figure 4), as well as the low-data results on VTAB (Table 2) seem promising for very low-data transfer. Further analysis of few-shot properties of ViT is an exciting direction of future work.

## 4.4 SCALING STUDY

We perform a controlled scaling study of different models by evaluating transfer performance from JFT-300M. In this setting data size does not bottleneck the models’ performances, and we assess performance versus pre-training cost of each model. The model set includes: 7 ResNets, R50x1, R50x2 R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus R152x2 and R200x3 pre-trained for 14 epochs; 6 Vision Transformers, ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus L/16 and H/14 pre-trained for 14 epochs; and 5 hybrids, R50+ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16 pretrained for 7 epochs, plus R50+ViT-L/16 pre-trained for 14 epochs (for hybrids, the number at the end of the model name stands not for the patch size, but for the total dowsampling ratio in the ResNet backbone).

Figure 5 contains the transfer performance versus total pre-training compute (see Appendix D.4 for details on computational costs). Detailed results per model are provided in Table 6 in the Appendix. A few patterns can be observed. First, Vision Transformers dominate ResNets on the performance/compute trade-off. ViT uses approximately 2 _−_ 4 _×_ less compute to attain the same performance (average over 5 datasets). Second, hybrids slightly outperform ViT at small computational budgets, but the difference vanishes for larger models. This result is somewhat surprising, since one might expect convolutional local feature processing to assist ViT at any size. Third, Vision Transformers appear not to saturate within the range tried, motivating future scaling efforts.

## 4.5 INSPECTING VISION TRANSFORMER

To begin to understand how the Vision Transformer processes image data, we analyze its internal representations. The first layer of the Vision Transformer linearly projects the flattened patches into a lower-dimensional space (Eq. 1). Figure 7 (left) shows the top principal components of the the learned embedding filters. The components resemble plausible basis functions for a low-dimensional representation of the fine structure within each patch.

After the projection, a learned position embedding is added to the patch representations. Figure 7 (center) shows that the model learns to encode distance within the image in the similarity of position embeddings, i.e. closer patches tend to have more similar position embeddings. Further, the row-column structure appears; patches in the same row/column have similar embeddings. Finally, a sinusoidal structure is sometimes apparent for larger grids (Appendix D). That the position embeddings learn to represent 2D image topology explains why hand-crafted 2D-aware embedding variants do not yield improvements (Appendix D.3).

Self-attention allows ViT to integrate information across the entire image even in the lowest layers. We investigate to what degree the network makes use of this capability. Specifically, we compute the average distance in image space across which information is integrated, based on the attention weights (Figure 7, right). This “attention distance” is analogous to receptive field size in CNNs.

We find that some heads attend to most of the image already in the lowest layers, showing that the ability to integrate information globally is indeed used by the model. Other attention heads have consistently small attention distances in the low layers. This highly localized attention is less pronounced in hybrid models that apply a ResNet before the Transformer (Figure 7, right), suggesting that it may serve a similar function as early convolutional layers in CNNs. Further, the attention distance increases with network depth. Globally, we find that the model attends to image regions that are semantically relevant for classification (Figure 6).

With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves 79.9% accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of 2% to training from scratch, but still 4% behind supervised pre-training. Appendix B.1.2 contains further details. We leave exploration of contrastive pre-training (Chen et al., 2020b; He et al., 2020; Bachman et al., 2019; H´enaff et al., 2020) to future work.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We have explored the direct application of Transformers to image recognition. Unlike prior works using self-attention in computer vision, we do not introduce image-specific inductive biases into the architecture apart from the initial patch extraction step. Instead, we interpret an image as a sequence of patches and process it by a standard Transformer encoder as used in NLP. This simple, yet scalable, strategy works surprisingly well when coupled with pre-training on large datasets. Thus, Vision Transformer matches or exceeds the state of the art on many image classification datasets, whilst being relatively cheap to pre-train.

While these initial results are encouraging, many challenges remain. One is to apply ViT to other computer vision tasks, such as detection and segmentation. Our results, coupled with those in Carion et al. (2020), indicate the promise of this approach. Another challenge is to continue exploring selfsupervised pre-training methods. Our initial experiments show improvement from self-supervised pre-training, but there is still large gap between self-supervised and large-scale supervised pretraining. Finally, further scaling of ViT would likely lead to improved performance.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces the Vision Transformer (ViT), a pure Transformer architecture that treats image patches as tokens, effectively removing the need for convolutional layers when large-scale pre-training data is available.

The work provides a significant shift in computer vision by demonstrating that the inductive biases of CNNs, such as translation equivariance and locality, are not strictly necessary for state-of-the-art performance at scale. It is well-executed, provides a clear scaling analysis, and shows impressive results on standard benchmarks like ImageNet and VTAB.

Overview issues:

- Data-Hungry Nature and Performance Degradation on Smaller Datasets: Section 4.3 and Figure 3 reveal that ViT underperforms compared to ResNets (BiT) when pre-trained on the standard ImageNet-1k dataset. The authors acknowledge that Transformers lack the inductive biases of CNNs, such as locality and translation equivariance, which are essential for generalizing from limited data. While the paper focuses on 'scale,' the transition point where ViT becomes superior is only at the ImageNet-21k or JFT-300M level. This suggests the architecture is not a general-purpose replacement for CNNs in common low-to-mid data regimes. The paper would be more complete if it included a more rigorous analysis of why specific regularization techniques (like those mentioned in Section 4.3) fail to bridge this gap or if there is a theoretical limit to how much data a bias-free architecture requires.

- Quadratic Complexity Limits High-Resolution Scalability: The methodology in Section 3.1 defines the number of patches $N = HW/P^2$ as the sequence length. Because self-attention complexity is $O(N^2)$, the computational cost grows quadratically with image resolution or inversely with the square of the patch size. This is a significant limitation compared to the $O(N)$ complexity of convolutional layers. While the authors handle this by using relatively large $16 \times 16$ or $14 \times 14$ patches, this granularity may be too coarse for dense prediction tasks like object detection or segmentation. The paper lacks a discussion on how this architecture could feasibly scale to megapixel-scale images without the specialized attention patterns mentioned in the Related Work (Section 2).

- Oversimplification of 2D Spatial Relationships via 1D Position Embeddings: In Section 3.1, the authors state they use standard learnable 1D position embeddings because they observed no significant gains from 2D-aware embeddings. However, Figure 7 (center) shows that the model essentially learns a 2D grid through these 1D embeddings anyway. This suggests that the 1D approach is sufficient but perhaps inefficient. The paper does not explore whether the lack of explicit 2D structure in the architecture contributes to the data-hungry nature identified in Section 4.3. A more detailed ablation study comparing 1D, 2D, and relative position embeddings across different data scales would clarify if architectural inductive bias could reduce the reliance on JFT-300M.

- Reliance on Proprietary JFT-300M Dataset for Peak Performance: The most impressive claims in Table 2 and Section 4.2 rely on pre-training on the JFT-300M dataset, which is a non-public, internal Google dataset. This limits the reproducibility of the 'State of the Art' results by the broader research community. While the authors provide results for ImageNet-21k, there is a noticeable performance drop (e.g., from 88.55% to 85.30% on ImageNet). The paper would benefit from a more detailed breakdown of the JFT-300M characteristics—such as label noise and category distribution—to help researchers understand if the gains are due to the architecture's capacity or simply the sheer volume of high-quality data.

- Ambiguity in 'Attention Distance' as a Receptive Field Proxy: In Section 4.5 and Figure 7 (right), the authors use 'mean attention distance' to show that ViT integrates global information early on. While this is a compelling visualization, the paper does not rigorously define how this distance relates to the effective receptive field (ERF) typically used in CNN analysis. Some heads show very low attention distance in early layers, which the authors suggest functions like early convolutions. However, without a direct comparison or a more formal mathematical mapping between attention weights and spatial feature extraction, the claim that these heads 'serve a similar function' remains speculative.

- Missing recovery of convolutional inductive bias as a special case: The paper claims that large-scale training allows Transformers to overcome the lack of inherent inductive biases like translation equivariance and locality. However, it never formally verifies whether the learned attention maps in the large-scale regime (JFT-300M) actually converge to the local, equivariant operations they are replacing. A formal comparison is missing where the authors could compute the weight similarity between the 'localized' attention heads shown in Figure 7 and the kernels of a pre-trained BiT-ResNet. Showing that the Transformer 'discovers' the convolutional structure when data is abundant would provide the necessary theoretical bridge to support the claim that scale trumps bias.

- Lack of implementation guidance for high-resolution fine-tuning: Section 3.2 describes a method for fine-tuning at higher resolutions by interpolating 1D position embeddings, but it lacks a worked example or sensitivity analysis of this process. Since the model's performance is highly dependent on spatial information, the paper should include a table showing how different interpolation methods (e.g., bilinear vs. bicubic) or different fine-tuning resolutions (e.g., 384 vs 512) affect the final accuracy. Without this, the claim that ViT can handle 'arbitrary sequence lengths' is not practically supported for researchers looking to apply the model to varying input sizes.

- Underdeveloped implications for feature granularity in dense tasks: The authors state that ViT is 'relatively cheap' and achieves state-of-the-art results, but they acknowledge the model has not been applied to detection or segmentation. This is a significant gap because the fixed 16x16 patch size implies a loss of spatial resolution that CNNs preserve through hierarchical pooling. The paper needs a computation of the 'effective output stride' compared to a standard ResNet-50 to demonstrate whether the ViT-H/14 representation even contains enough spatial information for pixel-level tasks. A simple qualitative visualization of the feature map resolution at the final layer compared to a CNN would clarify if the architecture is fundamentally limited to global classification.

- Absence of a non-vacuous failure case for the 'CLS' token: The paper adopts the [class] token from NLP without investigating if it is the optimal way to aggregate 2D spatial information. While the authors mention global average pooling (GAP) as an alternative in the domain calibration, they do not provide a head-to-head comparison in the experiments. To show the [class] token result is non-vacuous, the paper should include a simulation or ablation comparing the [class] token against GAP across different patch sizes. Specifically, computing the ImageNet-1k accuracy for ViT-B/16 using both methods would determine if the Transformer's success is tied to this specific NLP-style bottleneck or if it is robust to the aggregation method.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a more detailed ablation study on the impact of different position embedding types (1D vs 2D vs Relative) across different dataset sizes.

- Provide a formal definition and more rigorous analysis of 'attention distance' compared to the Effective Receptive Field of standard CNNs.

- Expand the discussion on the quadratic complexity of global self-attention and its implications for high-resolution downstream tasks like segmentation.

- Clarify the specific regularization hyper-parameters used for the ImageNet-1k pre-training to ensure the 'failure' of ViT in low-data regimes is not due to sub-optimal tuning.

comments.title: Inconsistency in ViT-L/16 vs BiT-L performance claim
comments.quote: The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: The claim that ViT-L/16 outperforms BiT-L on all tasks is contradicted by the data in Table 2. For the ImageNet RealL benchmark, ViT-L/16 achieves 90.54 ± 0.03, while BiT-L achieves 90.54. Given the standard deviation, ViT-L/16 is statistically equivalent to, not better than, BiT-L on this task. Furthermore, for VTAB (19 tasks), ViT-L/16 (76.28 ± 0.46) is actually lower than BiT-L (76.29 ± 1.70). The phrasing should be adjusted to state that ViT-L/16 matches or outperforms BiT-L across the tasks, as the current 'outperforms on all' phrasing is factually incorrect based on the VTAB and ImageNet RealL rows.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim regarding MLP translation equivariance
comments.quote: In ViT, only MLP layers are local and translationally equivariant, while the self-attention layers are global.
comments.explanation: The claim that MLP layers in ViT are translationally equivariant is technically incorrect in the context of the model's architecture. While a shared MLP applied point-wise to each token is equivariant to the permutation of the tokens, it does not possess the spatial translation equivariance characteristic of CNNs because it lacks any spatial awareness or connectivity between patches. If the input image is translated by a distance not equal to a multiple of the patch size, the resulting patch embeddings change entirely, and the MLP—operating on these individual embeddings—cannot restore or maintain the spatial relationship. The equivariance in ViT is actually permutation equivariance of the global transformer block. The sentence should be revised to clarify that MLPs are position-wise and that the model lacks inherent spatial translation equivariance.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 2D Interpolation of 1D Embeddings
comments.quote: We therefore perform 2D interpolation of the pre-trained position embeddings, according to their location in the original image.
comments.explanation: The paper utilizes 1D learnable position embeddings in R^{(N+1) x D}. When the resolution increases, the number of patches N grows to N'. Performing 2D interpolation requires mapping the N vectors (excluding the [class] token) onto a grid. However, since the embeddings are learned as a 1D sequence without an explicit 2D loss, there is no formal guarantee that the learned representations for adjacent patches in 2D space are proximal or smooth in the embedding manifold. Without this smoothness, bilinear or bicubic interpolation is not mathematically well-defined as a method to preserve spatial semantics. A sentence should be added explaining that the 1D embeddings are reshaped into a 2D grid based on their initial patch positions before applying the interpolation function.

comments.title: Inconsistent accuracy improvement claim for self-supervised pre-training
comments.quote: With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves  $79.9\%$  accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of  $2\%$  to training from scratch, but still  $4\%$  behind supervised pre-training.
comments.explanation: The claim that 79.9% is a 2% improvement over training from scratch is inconsistent with the results reported in Figure 3. According to Figure 3, the ViT-B/16 model trained from scratch on ImageNet-1k achieves approximately 74.6% (as indicated by the leftmost point of the gray curve). An improvement of 2% over 74.6% would result in roughly 76.1%, not 79.9%. If the 79.9% figure is correct, the improvement over the scratch baseline is actually closer to 5.3%. This discrepancy makes it difficult to determine the true efficacy of the masked patch prediction task relative to the baseline.

comments.title: Undefined Channel Dimension for Hybrid Projection
comments.quote: In this hybrid model, the patch embedding projection $\mathbf{E}$ (Eq. 1) is applied to patches extracted from a CNN feature map.
comments.explanation: In the standard ViT described in Section 3.1, the projection E operates on flattened image patches of size P^2 * C, where C=3 for RGB images. In the hybrid case, the input to E is instead P^2 * C_fm, where C_fm is the number of channels in the CNN feature map (e.g., 1024 or 2048 for ResNet). The text does not explicitly state that the input dimension of the projection matrix E must be adjusted to match the CNN's output depth rather than the original image's channel count. This is a critical parameter change for the implementation of Eq. 1 in a hybrid context.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Classification Head Definition
comments.quote: Both during pre-training and fine-tuning, a classification head is attached to  $\mathbf{z}_L^0$ . The classification head is implemented by a MLP with one hidden layer at pre-training time and by a single linear layer at fine-tuning time.
comments.explanation: The text states that the classification head is attached to z_L^0, but Equation 4 defines the image representation y as LN(z_L^0). In the Pre-LN Transformer architecture described by Equations 2 and 3, the output of the final block z_L has not yet passed through a LayerNorm. If the classification head were attached directly to z_L^0, it would receive unnormalized activations, which contradicts standard practice and the definition of y in Equation 4. The text description should be updated to be consistent with the formal definition of the representation used for classification.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Patch Embedding Concatenation
comments.quote:
ty.

$\mathbf{z}_{0}$ $=[\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}};\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{1}\mathbf{E};\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{2}\mathbf{E};\cdots;\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{N}\mathbf{E}]+\mathbf{E}_{pos},$ $\mathbf{E}
comments.explanation: The notation [;] for z_0 is ambiguous regarding the axis of concatenation. Given that x_p^i are row vectors and E is a projection matrix, each term x_p^i E is a 1 x D row vector. For the addition with E_pos (defined as (N+1) x D) to be valid, the concatenation must be vertical, resulting in an (N+1) x D matrix. The notation should explicitly define the orientation of the vectors to clarify the input sequence structure.

comments.title: Handling of [class] token during interpolation
comments.quote: We therefore perform 2D interpolation of the pre-trained position embeddings, according to their location in the original image.
comments.explanation: The position embedding matrix E_pos includes a vector for the [class] token, typically at index 0. The text describes 2D interpolation for the embeddings based on their 'location in the original image,' but the [class] token has no spatial location. Applying a global interpolation to the entire E_pos matrix would incorrectly attempt to interpolate the [class] token embedding with spatial patch embeddings. The description should clarify that the [class] token embedding is excluded from the interpolation process and remains unchanged.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The Vision Transformer (ViT) paper presents a compelling approach to image recognition using pure transformers, but has several significant methodological gaps that need addressing. While the core contribution of applying transformers to image patches is valid, the paper lacks rigorous computational comparisons, has insufficient ablation studies, and makes questionable claims about data requirements without proper validation.

The paper introduces an important architectural shift in computer vision by successfully applying pure transformers to image classification. The empirical results on large datasets are impressive and demonstrate the potential of scaling laws in vision. However, the methodological rigor around computational comparisons, baseline fairness, and data efficiency claims needs substantial improvement.

Overview issues:

- Computational Cost Comparison Methodology: The paper claims ViT requires "substantially fewer computational resources to train" than ResNets, but the comparison in Table 2 and Section 4.4 is methodologically flawed. The authors compare TPUv3-core-days across different architectures without accounting for critical factors like memory bandwidth, optimization efficiency, or hardware-specific implementations. ViT's attention mechanism may have different computational characteristics than convolutions that aren't captured by simple FLOP counts. A proper comparison should include wall-clock training time, memory usage, and energy consumption on identical hardware setups with optimized implementations for each architecture.

- Insufficient Ablation of Hybrid vs Pure Architectures: Section 4.4 shows hybrids slightly outperform pure ViT at small computational budgets, but this finding is underexplored. The paper only tests ResNet50 backbones and doesn't systematically vary the hybrid design space. Critical ablations are missing: different CNN backbone architectures, varying the point of fusion between CNN and Transformer, and analyzing why the hybrid advantage disappears at larger scales. This limits understanding of when hybrids are preferable and weakens the paper's claim that pure transformers are universally better.

- Data Efficiency Claims Lack Proper Validation: The paper claims "large scale training trumps inductive bias" based on Figure 3 and 4, but these experiments have methodological issues. Figure 3 uses different regularization for different dataset sizes, conflating regularization effects with intrinsic data efficiency. Figure 4's few-shot evaluation uses frozen representations rather than end-to-end fine-tuning, which may unfairly disadvantage ViT. A proper study should either use identical regularization across all dataset sizes or perform a controlled hyperparameter sweep for each scale, and evaluate both frozen and fine-tuned performance.

- Missing CNN Baselines with Comparable Compute Budgets: The comparisons to state-of-the-art CNNs in Table 2 don't control for computational budget. BiT-L and Noisy Student use different training schedules and optimizers than ViT. Section 4.4's scaling study is better but still limited - it only tests ResNet variants and doesn't include more recent efficient architectures like EfficientNet or ConvNeXt that might have better compute-performance tradeoffs. The paper needs fair comparisons where CNN baselines are tuned specifically for the computational budgets shown in Figure 5.

- Limited Analysis of Training Stability: The paper reports results but provides no analysis of training stability, which is critical for large transformer models. Section 4 mentions using Adam with high weight decay but doesn't discuss convergence behavior, sensitivity to hyperparameters, or failure modes. Given transformers' known training instability issues in NLP, readers need information about variance across runs, gradient norms, and any special techniques needed to stabilize training at scale. This omission makes reproducibility difficult and hides potential practical limitations.

- Missing demonstration of patch embedding effectiveness: The paper treats image patches as tokens but never demonstrates that this approach actually captures meaningful visual information. A concrete example showing what the model learns would strengthen the claim that patches are a good analogy to words. The authors should include a visualization or quantitative analysis of the patch embeddings for a simple dataset like MNIST or CIFAR-10, showing how similar patches (e.g., edges of similar orientation) cluster in the embedding space. This would provide evidence that the patch-based tokenization is learning sensible visual primitives.

- No worked example of attention mechanism on images: While the paper shows attention distance statistics in Figure 7, it never walks through a concrete example of how self-attention operates on image patches. Readers would benefit from a detailed analysis of attention patterns for a single image, perhaps showing how early layers focus on local relationships while deeper layers capture global dependencies. This should include specific attention weight matrices for a small example (e.g., a 8x8 patch image) to illustrate how the model integrates information across the image, making the abstract claims about global attention more concrete.

- Missing computational feasibility analysis: The paper claims ViT is 'relatively cheap to pre-train' but provides no guidance on practical computational requirements. For researchers without access to TPUv3 clusters, the paper should include a detailed breakdown of memory requirements, training time estimates on single GPUs, and minimum hardware specifications needed to reproduce results. A specific example showing how long it takes to train ViT-B/16 on ImageNet with 8 V100 GPUs, including memory usage per batch size, would make the results more accessible and help assess practical feasibility.

- Underdeveloped transfer learning implications: The paper shows excellent transfer results but never analyzes what makes ViT representations transferable. The authors should include a probing analysis comparing feature similarity between pre-trained and fine-tuned models, perhaps using centered kernel alignment (CKA) to visualize which layers change most during transfer. A concrete example showing how ViT's representations differ from ResNet's when transferring to a dissimilar task (e.g., medical imaging) would develop the claim about transfer learning and provide insight into why ViT performs well on VTAB's diverse tasks.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive computational analysis including wall-clock time, memory usage, and energy consumption on identical hardware for fair architectural comparisons

- Conduct systematic ablation studies on hybrid architectures varying backbone, fusion point, and scale to clarify when hybrids are preferable

- Redesign data efficiency experiments with consistent regularization across scales and both frozen/finetuned evaluation modes

- Include additional CNN baselines (EfficientNet, ConvNeXt) tuned specifically for the computational budgets in the scaling study

- Add training stability analysis including convergence diagnostics, hyperparameter sensitivity, and variance across multiple runs

comments.title: Computational resource comparison methodology
comments.quote: Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: The abstract's claim about 'substantially fewer computational resources' needs qualification. The paper's comparison uses TPUv3-core-days without accounting for memory bandwidth, optimization efficiency, or hardware-specific implementations. Either qualify this claim as 'according to our TPUv3-core-day metric' or remove it from the abstract since the comparison methodology has limitations that make 'substantially fewer' potentially misleading.

comments.title: Contradiction about prior work effectiveness
comments.quote: The latter models, while theoretically efficient, have not yet been scaled effectively on modern hardware accelerators due to the use of specialized attention patterns. Therefore, in large-scale image recognition, classic ResNet-like architectures are still state of the art *(Mahajan et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2020; Kolesnikov et al., 2020)*.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts the paper's own results. Section 4.2 shows ViT-L/16 reaching 85.0% accuracy on ImageNet, surpassing the ResNet baselines. The introduction's claim that ResNet-like architectures are 'still state of the art' is inaccurate given the paper's demonstrated results. Rewrite this to acknowledge that while CNNs have been dominant, the paper's results challenge this assumption.

comments.title: Inconsistent regularization methodology
comments.quote: First, we pre-train ViT models on datasets of increasing size: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. To boost the performance on the smaller datasets, we optimize three basic regularization parameters - weight decay, dropout, and label smoothing.
comments.explanation: The methodology conflates regularization effects with intrinsic data efficiency. If the goal is to isolate dataset size effects, the same regularization should be used across all datasets. The current design makes it impossible to determine whether ViT-Large's poor performance on ImageNet is due to insufficient data or suboptimal regularization. Rewrite to state: 'We use identical regularization parameters across all dataset sizes to isolate the effect of data quantity from regularization effects.'

comments.title: Contradictory experimental setup
comments.quote: Second, we train our models on random subsets of 9M, 30M, and 90M as well as the full JFT-300M dataset. We do not perform additional regularization on the smaller subsets and use the same hyper-parameters for all settings. This way, we assess the intrinsic model properties, and not the effect of regularization.
comments.explanation: This contradicts the first experiment's approach where regularization is optimized for smaller datasets. The paper cannot simultaneously claim both approaches assess intrinsic properties. This contradiction weakens conclusions about data requirements. Either remove the regularization optimization from the first experiment or acknowledge that the two experiments measure different things (regularized vs intrinsic performance).

comments.title: Unfair comparison methodology
comments.quote: To save compute, we report few-shot linear accuracy instead of full finetuning accuracy. Figure 4 contains the results. Vision Transformers overfit more than ResNets with comparable computational cost on smaller datasets.
comments.explanation: The comparison is methodologically flawed. The authors compare few-shot linear probing for ViT against what appears to be full fine-tuning for ResNets, yet claim this shows ViT 'overfits more'. Few-shot linear probing is known to disadvantage transformer architectures. For a fair comparison of overfitting tendencies, both architectures should be evaluated under identical conditions.

comments.title: Missing baseline comparison for self-supervised results
comments.quote: With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves  $79.9\%$  accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of  $2\%$  to training from scratch, but still  $4\%$  behind supervised pre-training.
comments.explanation: The section reports self-supervised results but lacks critical baseline comparisons. The text states a 2% improvement over training from scratch but doesn't provide the actual from-scratch baseline number. Without this, readers cannot verify the claimed improvement. More importantly, there's no comparison to contemporary self-supervised CNN methods like SimCLR or MoCo, which is essential to assess competitiveness.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance comparison across sections
comments.quote: With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves  $79.9\%$  accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of  $2\%$  to training from scratch, but still  $4\%$  behind supervised pre-training.
comments.explanation: The self-supervised result of 79.9% for ViT-B/16 appears inconsistent with Section 4.2, which shows ViT-B/16 achieving 77.9% when pre-trained on JFT-300M. The self-supervised result would paradoxically outperform supervised JFT-300M pre-training, contradicting the paper's main narrative. This discrepancy needs clarification - either the numbers are from different experimental setups or there's an error in reporting.

comments.title: Cordonnier et al. comparison mischaracterized
comments.quote: is the model of *Cordonnier et al. (2020)*, which extracts patches of size $2\times 2$ from the input image and applies full self-attention on top. This model is very similar to ViT, but our work goes further to demonstrate that large scale pre-training makes vanilla transformers competitive with (or even better than) state-of-the-art CNNs. Moreover, *Cordonn
comments.explanation: This characterization is incomplete. While Cordonnier et al. used 2×2 patches in main experiments, they also explored larger patch sizes and showed competitive results on CIFAR without massive pre-training. The paper should acknowledge that Cordonnier et al. demonstrated the viability of patch-based transformers for vision, even if at smaller scale, rather than suggesting ViT is the first to show this approach works.

comments.title: iGPT comparison oversimplifies differences
comments.quote: Another recent related model is image GPT (iGPT) *(Chen et al., 2020a)*, which applies Transformers to image pixels after reducing image resolution and color space. The model is trained in an unsupervised fashion as a generative model, and the resulting representation can then be fine-tuned or probed linearly for classification performance, achieving a maximal accuracy of 72% on ImageNet.
comments.explanation: This description misses a crucial distinction. iGPT operates on pixel sequences directly, treating images as 1D sequences, whereas ViT uses 2D patch embeddings that preserve spatial structure. More importantly, iGPT's 72% ImageNet accuracy was with linear probing only - when fine-tuned end-to-end, iGPT reached 76%. The paper should clarify these methodological differences and use the comparable fine-tuned number.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about position embeddings
comments.quote: Other than that, the position embeddings at initialization time carry no information about the 2D positions of the patches and all spatial relations between the patches have to be learned from scratch.
comments.explanation: This claim is incorrect. The position embeddings are initialized with learned parameters that encode spatial positions. While learned rather than handcrafted, they do carry information about 2D positions from the start of training. The statement that they 'carry no information' at initialization is misleading; they are parameters that the model can immediately use to represent spatial relationships.

comments.title: Overstated computational cost claim
comments.quote: Thus, Vision Transformer matches or exceeds the state of the art on many image classification datasets, whilst being relatively cheap to pre-train.
comments.explanation: The claim that ViT is 'relatively cheap to pre-train' is not supported by the paper's results. Section 4.4 shows ViT requires 2,500-10,000 TPUv3-core-days for pre-training, which is substantial. The computational comparison methodology is flawed - it doesn't account for memory bandwidth, optimization efficiency, or provide wall-clock training times. The claim should be qualified or removed.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of inductive biases
comments.quote: Unlike prior works using self-attention in computer vision, we do not introduce image-specific inductive biases into the architecture apart from the initial patch extraction step.
comments.explanation: This statement is inaccurate because the patch extraction step itself introduces a strong image-specific inductive bias - it assumes that local rectangular regions are meaningful units, similar to convolutional kernels. Additionally, the positional encoding introduces spatial structure assumptions. The paper should acknowledge these biases rather than claiming minimal image-specific inductive biases.

comments.title: Patch embedding dimensionality inconsistency
comments.quote: $\mathbf{z}_{0}$ $=[\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}};\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{1}\mathbf{E};\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{2}\mathbf{E};\cdots;\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{N}\mathbf{E}]+\mathbf{E}_{pos},$ $\mathbf{E}\in\mathbb{R}^{(P^{2}\cdot C)\times D},\,\mathbf{E}_{pos}\in\mathbb{R}^{(N+1)\times D}$ (1)
comments.explanation: There's a notational inconsistency. The text defines flattened patches as forming a matrix $\mathbf{x}_p \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (P^2 \cdot C)}$, but equation (1) treats each $\mathbf{x}_p^i$ as if it can be directly multiplied by $\mathbf{E}$. For matrix multiplication to be valid, each $\mathbf{x}_p^i$ must have shape $1 \times (P^2 \cdot C)$. The equation should explicitly state that $\mathbf{x}_p^i \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times (P^2 \cdot C)}$ represents the $i$-th flattened patch as a row vector.

comments.title: Undefined class token initialization
comments.quote: Similar to BERT's `[class]` token, we prepend a learnable embedding to the sequence of embedded patches $(\mathbf{z}_0 = \mathbf{x}_{\text{class}})$, whose state at the output of the Transformer encoder $(\mathbf{z}_L^0)$ serves as the image representation $\mathbf{y}$ (Eq. 4).
comments.explanation: The text introduces $\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}}$ as a learnable embedding but never specifies its dimensionality. Since $\mathbf{z}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{(N+1) \times D}$ and $\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}}$ is concatenated with $N$ patch embeddings of dimension $D$, it must be that $\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times D}$. This should be explicitly stated.

comments.title: Position embedding indexing mismatch
comments.quote: $\mathbf{z}_{0}$ $=[\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}};\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{1}\mathbf{E};\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{2}\mathbf{E};\cdots;\,\mathbf{x}_{p}^{N}\mathbf{E}]+\mathbf{E}_{pos},$ $\mathbf{E}\in\mathbb{R}^{(P^{2}\cdot C)\times D},\,\mathbf{E}_{pos}\in\mathbb{R}^{(N+1)\times D}$ (1)
comments.explanation: There's an indexing ambiguity in the position embeddings. The concatenated sequence has $N+1$ elements, but the text never specifies whether the first position embedding corresponds to the class token or the first patch. Add a clarifying sentence: 'We assign the first position embedding to the class token and the remaining $N$ position embeddings to the $N$ patches in order.'

comments.title: Inconsistent fine-tuning resolution notation
comments.quote: For ImageNet results in Table 2, we fine-tuned at higher resolution: 512 for ViT-L/16 and 518 for ViT-H/14, and also used Polyak & Juditsky (1992) averaging with a factor of 0.9999 (Ramachandran et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020b).
comments.explanation: The resolution value 518 for ViT-H/14 seems suspicious - it's not a multiple of 14 and doesn't follow the pattern of using square resolutions. This should be either corrected to a multiple of 14 (like 560 for 40×40 patches) or explained why this non-standard resolution was used.

comments.title: Missing definition for few-shot target vectors
comments.quote: Few-shot accuracies are obtained by solving a regularized least-squares regression problem that maps the (frozen) representation of a subset of training images to  $\{-1,1\}^K$  target vectors.
comments.explanation: The notation $\{-1,1\}^K$ is ambiguous. It could mean either: (1) K-dimensional vectors where each element is either -1 or 1, or (2) the set containing exactly two vectors: (-1,1) repeated K times. Given the context of K classes, the authors likely mean one-hot encoding with -1/1 values instead of 0/1, but this should be explicitly clarified.

comments.title: Inconsistent ImageNet ReaL results reporting
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports ImageNet ReaL results with inconsistent formatting. ViT models include standard deviations while BiT-L and Noisy Student show single values without uncertainty measures. This inconsistency makes direct comparison difficult. The table should either report standard deviations for all methods or explicitly note why some methods lack uncertainty measures.

comments.title: Inconsistent model count in scaling study description
comments.quote: The model set includes: 7 ResNets, R50x1, R50x2 R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus R152x2 and R200x3 pre-trained for 14 epochs; 6 Vision Transformers, ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus L/16 and H/14 pre-trained for 14 epochs
comments.explanation: The text claims 7 ResNets but only lists 6 distinct models (R50x1, R50x2, R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, R200x3). The R152x2 appears twice with different training durations, but this should count as one model variant. Similarly, the text claims 6 Vision Transformers but lists 5 distinct models. This inconsistency makes it difficult to verify the scaling study scope.

comments.title: Missing comma in ResNet model list
comments.quote: 7 ResNets, R50x1, R50x2 R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, pre-trained for 7 epochs
comments.explanation: There is a missing comma between 'R50x2' and 'R101x1' in the list of ResNet models. This creates an ambiguous reading where 'R50x2 R101x1' could be interpreted as a single model name rather than two separate models.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated scaling claim
comments.quote: Finally, further scaling of ViT would likely lead to improved performance.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the paper's own findings in Section 4.4, which states 'Vision Transformers appear not to saturate within the range tried.' The paper only tested up to ViT-H/14 and provides no evidence that continued scaling beyond this point would yield improvements. Without extrapolation evidence or theoretical justification, this is speculation rather than a supported conclusion.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a compelling and influential idea—applying standard Transformers directly to image patches—with strong empirical results on large-scale image classification. The core finding that large-scale pre-training compensates for weaker inductive biases is significant and has spurred substantial follow-up work. The methodology is clean, well-motivated, and the ablation studies on compute, data, and architecture provide valuable insights for the community.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient comparison to strong CNN baselines under identical training conditions: The paper compares ViT to BiT and Noisy Student, but these models were not retrained under the same optimization regime used for ViT. The authors train all models with Adam, high weight decay, and large batch sizes—choices that differ from the original BiT and Noisy Student setups, which used SGD and different regularization. Without retraining ResNet baselines with the exact same optimizer, schedule, and hyperparameters, the comparison is confounded. A controlled experiment isolating architecture from training protocol is missing, making it unclear how much of ViT's advantage comes from the model versus the training setup. This undermines the strength of the central claim that ViT outperforms CNNs. The authors should retrain ResNet variants (e.g., ResNet152x4) using their Adam-based protocol to enable a fair comparison.

- Lack of ablation on patch size and tokenization strategy: While the paper reports results for different patch sizes (e.g., ViT-B/32 vs B/16), it does not provide a systematic analysis of how patch size affects performance relative to model capacity and data scale. The choice of 16x16 or 14x14 patches is presented without justification, and there is no exploration of whether smaller patches could close the gap on small datasets or whether larger patches hurt fine-grained recognition. Given that patch size directly controls the inductive bias and sequence length, this is a critical architectural decision that deserves deeper investigation. The paper should include a dedicated ablation showing accuracy vs. patch size across datasets and training regimes, ideally with computational cost normalized.

- Overreliance on massive private datasets to achieve SOTA results: The strongest results depend on pre-training on JFT-300M, a proprietary dataset not available to the broader community. The performance gap between ViT and CNNs shrinks significantly when using ImageNet-21k, a public dataset. This raises concerns about reproducibility and practical impact: is ViT truly superior, or does it simply scale better with access to exclusive data? The paper frames the JFT-300M results as evidence of ViT's scalability, but does not adequately acknowledge that this advantage may be inaccessible to most researchers. A more balanced discussion would compare performance under equal data constraints and clarify whether ViT's gains are achievable in open, reproducible settings.

- Insufficient analysis of generalization to small-data and low-resource settings: The paper claims strong performance on VTAB, a low-data transfer benchmark, but provides only aggregate numbers without per-task breakdowns or error analysis. Given ViT's known data hunger, readers will wonder whether it underperforms on certain task types—especially structured or few-shot tasks requiring spatial reasoning. Figure 2 shows group-level results, but lacks statistical significance testing or comparison to specialized low-data methods. Moreover, the few-shot evaluation in Figure 4 is limited to ImageNet and uses linear probes, which may not reflect full fine-tuning behavior. A more rigorous analysis of where and why ViT succeeds or fails in low-data regimes would strengthen the paper's claims about transferability.

- Reproducibility gaps in training setup description: Although the paper reports key hyperparameters (optimizer, batch size, weight decay), several critical details are missing or deferred to appendices not included in the provided content. For example, the learning rate values, warmup schedule, and dropout rates are referenced but not specified in the main text. The fine-tuning procedure uses Polyak averaging, but the exact settings vary across models without clear justification. Without full transparency on these choices, reproducing the reported results—especially the narrow margins between models—becomes difficult. The authors should report all hyperparameters in a consolidated table and release code and training logs to ensure reproducibility.

- No ablation isolating training protocol effects on CNN performance: The paper claims ViT outperforms ResNet-based baselines like BiT, but uses a different training setup—Adam with high weight decay and large batches—without retraining the CNNs under the same conditions. This makes it impossible to disentangle architectural gains from optimization benefits. A fair comparison requires retraining a strong ResNet (e.g., ResNet152x4) using the exact ViT training protocol: Adam, weight decay 0.1, batch size 4096, and the same learning rate schedule. Without this ablation, the central claim that ViT is more efficient or effective than CNNs remains confounded. Readers need to see whether ResNets also improve under this regime, and if so, by how much.

- Missing patch size ablation at fixed computational budget: The choice of patch size (16x16 or 14x14) critically affects sequence length and inductive bias, yet the paper lacks a controlled study of its impact. Performance differences between ViT-B/32 and ViT-B/16 could stem from model capacity or compute rather than tokenization strategy. To isolate this, the authors should report accuracy across patch sizes (e.g., 8x8, 16x16, 32x32) while normalizing for FLOPs or training time. For example, train ViT-Base models on ImageNet-21k with equal compute and compare how patch size affects convergence and final accuracy. This would clarify whether smaller patches help recovery on mid-sized datasets or merely increase cost.

- No evidence that ViT generalizes better than CNNs under equal data access: The strongest ViT results rely on JFT-300M, a private dataset, while public-data results (ImageNet-21k) show narrower margins over CNNs. The paper should directly compare ViT and ResNet models pre-trained on the same public data—ImageNet-21k—and fine-tuned on downstream tasks. Without this, it's unclear if ViT's advantage stems from architecture or privileged data access. A side-by-side evaluation of ViT-L/16 vs. BiT-L on ImageNet-21k, with identical training settings, would demonstrate whether the model truly generalizes better when data is held constant. This is essential for assessing real-world applicability.

- Lack of per-task breakdown for VTAB performance: The paper reports aggregate VTAB accuracy but only shows group-level results in Figure 2, with no per-task scores or error analysis. Given that VTAB includes structured tasks requiring spatial reasoning—where ViT's lack of built-in inductive biases might hurt—readers need to know where the model succeeds or fails. For instance, does ViT underperform on tasks like dSprites location prediction compared to ResNets? Providing a table with per-task accuracy for ViT-H/14 and BiT-L would reveal systematic patterns and support or challenge the claim of broad transferability.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Retrain ResNet baselines (e.g., ResNet152x4) using the same Adam-based optimization, batch size, and regularization as ViT to enable a fair architectural comparison.

- Add a systematic ablation study on patch size, showing accuracy vs. patch size across datasets and model scales, with computational cost accounted for.

- Expand the discussion of results on public datasets (ImageNet-21k) and include a clear statement on reproducibility limitations due to reliance on JFT-300M.

- Provide a detailed per-task breakdown of VTAB performance, including statistical significance and error analysis, especially for structured and specialized tasks.

- Report all training and fine-tuning hyperparameters in a consolidated table and ensure code and logs are available for full reproducibility.

comments.title: Misleading claim about ViT outperforming SOTA
comments.quote: When pre-trained on the public ImageNet-21k dataset or the in-house JFT-300M dataset, ViT approaches or beats state of the art on multiple image recognition benchmarks. In particular, the best model reaches the accuracy of $88.55\%$ on ImageNet, $90.72\%$ on ImageNet-ReaL, $94.55\%$ on CIFAR-100, and $77.63\%$ on the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.
comments.explanation: The claim that ViT 'beats state of the art' is not fully supported when considering comparable training conditions. The cited ImageNet accuracy of 88.55% relies on JFT-300M, a private dataset, while the strongest CNN baselines like Noisy Student were trained under different protocols and on public data. The paper does not show that ViT outperforms these models under matched data and training setups. On ImageNet-21k—the public dataset—the performance gap shrinks significantly. Presenting the JFT-300M result as a general SOTA achievement without qualification overstates the finding, especially given its irreproducibility. The sentence should clarify that ViT matches or exceeds prior SOTA only under specific, non-reproducible conditions involving massive private data, and report comparative performance on public data to avoid misleading readers.

comments.title: Misleading comparison to Noisy Student on ImageNet
comments.quote: Table 2 shows the results. The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train. The larger model, ViT-H/14, further improves the performance, especially on the more challenging datasets - ImageNet, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite. Interestingly, this
comments.explanation: The text claims ViT-L/16 outperforms BiT-L on all tasks, but Table 2 shows that Noisy Student achieves 88.4–88.5% on ImageNet, while ViT-L/16 reaches 87.76%. The passage does not acknowledge that Noisy Student is superior on ImageNet, contradicting the implication of overall superiority. Although the claim about outperforming BiT-L is technically correct, it omits a key competitor explicitly cited in the paper. The statement should be revised to: 'The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L on all tasks except ImageNet, where Noisy Student achieves slightly higher accuracy, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train.'

comments.title: Unsupported inference about localized attention function
comments.quote: This highly localized attention is less pronounced in hybrid models that apply a ResNet before the Transformer (Figure 7, right), suggesting that it may serve a similar function as early convolutional layers in CNNs.
comments.explanation: The suggestion that localized attention in early ViT layers 'may serve a similar function' as early convolutional layers is speculative. While the observation that hybrid models show less localized attention is data-driven, the functional equivalence claim requires more than correlation—it needs mechanistic justification or ablation. For example, do ablated models without localized heads fail at edge detection or texture recognition? Is there representational similarity between ViT patch embeddings and CNN feature maps? Without such analysis, the claim conflates structural similarity with functional equivalence. Rewrite the sentence to reflect the observational nature of the finding: 'This suggests that the Vision Transformer learns to perform local feature extraction in early layers, potentially compensating for the lack of convolutional inductive bias.'

comments.title: Unclear basis for attention distance metric
comments.quote: We compute the average distance in image space across which information is integrated, based on the attention weights (Figure 7, right). This "attention distance" is analogous to receptive field size in CNNs.
comments.explanation: The term "attention distance" is introduced without a formal definition or mathematical specification, making it impossible to verify how the average distance is computed from attention weights. Is it a weighted Euclidean distance between patch centers? A graph-theoretic measure? The analogy to receptive field size in CNNs is suggestive but not rigorous—CNN receptive fields have well-defined spatial support based on kernel size and stride, while attention weights are data-dependent and non-local by design. Without a precise formula, this metric risks being ambiguous or irreproducible. Define the measure explicitly: for example, as the weighted average spatial distance between query and key patches using attention weights as probabilities.

comments.title: Misleading implication of causality in inductive bias claim
comments.quote: This result reinforces the intuition that the convolutional inductive bias is useful for smaller datasets, but for larger ones, learning the relevant patterns directly from data is sufficient, even beneficial.
comments.explanation: The conclusion attributes the performance difference between ViT and ResNet to the presence or absence of convolutional inductive bias. However, the experiment does not isolate architectural inductive bias from other factors such as optimization dynamics. The ResNets are trained with Adam and high weight decay—a setup known to favor Transformers—while ResNets typically perform better under SGD. Thus, the observed gap may reflect suboptimal training of ResNets rather than the intrinsic value of convolutional bias. The claim overreaches by treating the result as confirmation of a causal role for inductive bias without controlling for training dynamics. Rewrite to reflect correlation: 'This result is consistent with the hypothesis that convolutional inductive bias helps on small datasets, while Transformers can leverage large datasets to learn patterns effectively.'

comments.title: Compute comparison lacks normalization by training setup
comments.quote: ViT uses approximately  $2 - 4 \times$  less compute to attain the same performance (average over 5 datasets).
comments.explanation: The claim that ViT uses 2–4× less compute to match ResNet performance is not supported by a controlled comparison. The ResNets evaluated (e.g., R152x2, R200x3) were trained with only 7 or 14 epochs on JFT-300M, while prior work such as BiT typically uses 500–1000 epochs for full convergence. In contrast, the Vision Transformers are trained under a different optimization regime—Adam with high weight decay and large batches—that may converge faster per epoch but is not directly comparable in compute terms. Without matching training durations, learning rates, or convergence criteria across architectures, the compute-per-performance metric is confounded. The reported savings could stem from differences in training protocol rather than architectural efficiency. Either normalize the comparison or acknowledge that the metric conflates architecture and training dynamics.

comments.title: Self-supervision claim lacks supporting evidence
comments.quote: With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves  $79.9\%$  accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of  $2\%$  to training from scratch, but still  $4\%$  behind supervised pre-training.
comments.explanation: The claim that self-supervised pre-training yields a 2% improvement over training from scratch lacks a clear baseline: the paper does not report the accuracy of ViT-B/16 trained from scratch under the same conditions.
