Without this number, the 79.9% result cannot be contextualized as a 2% gain. The comparison may be to a different model or training setup, making the improvement ambiguous. The text should either cite the scratch-trained performance explicitly or remove the percentage improvement claim until supported. Rewrite the sentence to state both results directly: 'ViT-B/16 achieves 79.9% accuracy with self-supervised pre-training and X% from scratch.'

comments.title: Missing acknowledgment of dataset license and access restrictions
comments.quote: We thank many colleagues at Google for their help, in particular Andreas Steiner for crucial help with the infrastructure and the open-source release of the code; Joan Puigcerver and Maxim Neumann for help with the large-scale training infrastructure; Dmitry Lepikhin, Aravindh Mahendran, Daniel Keysers, Mario Lučić, Noam Shazeer, and Colin Raffel for useful discussions.
comments.explanation: The acknowledgments mention technical support and code release but omit any discussion of data access constraints, particularly regarding JFT-300M, which is central to the paper's strongest results. Given that this dataset is proprietary and not available to external researchers, its use raises reproducibility and fairness concerns that should be acknowledged. The current text presents the work as openly reproducible due to the code release, but fails to clarify that full replication is impossible without access to the training data. Add a sentence: 'We acknowledge that the JFT-300M dataset used in this work is not publicly available and that this limits full reproducibility by the broader community.'

comments.title: Class token embedding not properly distinguished
comments.quote: $\mathbf{z}_{0} = [\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}}; \mathbf{x}_{p}^{1}\mathbf{E}; \mathbf{x}_{p}^{2}\mathbf{E}; \dots; \mathbf{x}_{p}^{N}\mathbf{E}] + \mathbf{E}_{pos},$ $\mathbf{E}\in\mathbb{R}^{(P^{2}\cdot C)\times D},\,\mathbf{E}_{pos}\in\mathbb{R}^{(N+1)\times D}$ (1)
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}}$ in Equation (1) is ambiguous: the text states it is a 'learnable embedding', yet it appears concatenated with terms $\mathbf{x}_p^i\mathbf{E}$ that are projections of patches via $\mathbf{E}$. Since $\mathbf{x}_p^i \in \mathbb{R}^{P^2 \cdot C}$, the projection $\mathbf{x}_p^i\mathbf{E}$ maps to $\mathbb{R}^D$. For consistency, $\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}}$ must already be in $\mathbb{R}^D$, not in the patch space. However, the current notation suggests $\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}}$ is of the same type as unprojected patches, which is incorrect. There is no trainable projection applied to the class token—it is a standalone parameter. Replace $\mathbf{x}_{\text{class}}$ with $\mathbf{E}_{\text{class}} \in \mathbb{R}^D$ to reflect the correct parameterization: $\mathbf{z}_{0} = [\mathbf{E}_{\text{class}}; \mathbf{x}_{p}^{1}\mathbf{E}; \dots; \mathbf{x}_{p}^{N}\mathbf{E}] + \mathbf{E}_{\text{pos}}$.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for class token across layers
comments.quote: $\mathbf{y}$ $=\operatorname{LN}(\mathbf{z}_{L}^{0})$
comments.explanation: Equation (4) uses $\mathbf{z}_L^0$, denoting the first element (class token) of the final hidden state $\mathbf{z}_L$, but the superscript 0 is not defined in the preceding equations. While $\mathbf{z}_\ell$ is a matrix in $\mathbb{R}^{(N+1)\times D}$, with rows corresponding to tokens, the notation $\mathbf{z}_\ell^0$ implies a decomposition not introduced earlier. The prime notation $\mathbf{z}'_\ell$ is used for intermediate states, but no parallel indexing is given for token positions. This creates ambiguity: is $^0$ an index or part of the variable name? To resolve this, either define $\mathbf{z}_\ell^0$ explicitly as the class token embedding at layer $\ell$, or use standard indexing: rewrite Equation (4) as $\mathbf{y} = \operatorname{LN}(\mathbf{z}_L)[0]$, where $[0]$ denotes the first row. The latter improves clarity and aligns with array indexing conventions in machine learning literature.

comments.title: Patch ordering and position indexing unspecified
comments.quote: Position embeddings are added to the patch embeddings to retain positional information. We use standard learnable 1D position embeddings, since we have not observed significant performance gains from using more advanced 2D-aware position embeddings (Appendix D.3). The resulting sequence of embedding vectors serves as input to the encoder.
comments.explanation: While the use of 1D position embeddings is empirically justified in Appendix D.3, the mapping from 2D spatial layout to 1D sequence order is not specified. The patches form a grid of size $H/P \times W/P$, and their linearization into a sequence of length $N$ requires a convention (e.g., row-major order). The position embedding $\mathbf{E}_{\text{pos}} \in \mathbb{R}^{(N+1)\times D}$ assigns a unique vector to each of the $N+1$ tokens, including the class token. Without stating the scanning order, the model's behavior under spatial transformations (e.g., rotations, reflections) is ambiguous. Moreover, the class token's position must be fixed—typically at index 0—but this is not declared. Add a sentence specifying that patches are ordered in row-major scan and that the class token is assigned to position 0 in the sequence.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Vision Transformer (ViT), a straightforward adaptation of the standard Transformer architecture for image classification. By treating image patches as tokens and relying on large-scale supervised pre-training, the authors demonstrate that ViT matches or exceeds the performance of contemporary CNNs on several benchmarks while requiring less pre-training compute. The work successfully challenges the assumption that convolutional inductive biases are strictly necessary for high-performing vision models.

The core contribution is a clean, empirically driven demonstration that patch-based tokenization combined with massive datasets can rival convolutional networks for image recognition. The authors excel at isolating architectural simplicity from engineering complexity and provide a compelling scaling analysis that highlights the data hunger of attention-based models. The paper's strength lies in its transparent reporting of compute costs and its clear articulation of how dataset size interacts with model capacity.

Overview issues:

- Comparing purely supervised ViT models against Noisy Student in Table 2 creates a fundamental mismatch: Table 2 pits supervised ViT variants against Noisy Student, which leverages semi-supervised learning on unlabeled JFT-300M data. This comparison inflates the perceived performance gap and obscures whether the architecture itself drives the results or if the semi-supervised signal is the real differentiator. Readers will question the fairness of the benchmark when the training objectives and data usage differ so drastically. The authors should either match the protocol by training ViT with the same semi-supervised objectives or explicitly separate supervised and semi-supervised baselines. Without this control, the claim that ViT beats state of the art lacks methodological rigor.

- The training protocol description in Section 4.1 omits the data augmentation pipelines: Section 4.1 details the optimizer settings and weight decay values but leaves the data augmentation strategy entirely unmentioned. Vision Transformers are highly sensitive to augmentation strength, often requiring techniques like RandAugment or Mixup to stabilize training on smaller datasets. Assuming that standard CNN augmentations transfer directly without testing their impact undermines reproducibility and masks a key hyperparameter. Adding a systematic ablation on augmentation type and intensity would clarify how much of ViT's performance relies on architectural design versus training tricks. This omission is particularly critical given the paper's emphasis on compute efficiency and transfer learning.

- The compute metrics in Section 4.4 cannot isolate architectural efficiency from training duration: The scaling study reports performance versus TPUv3-core-days, yet the models were trained for different epoch counts and used varying learning rate schedules. Core-days alone cannot decouple architectural efficiency from differences in convergence speed or optimizer stability. A fair comparison requires normalizing by wall-clock time or exact FLOPs while holding training duration and hyperparameter search budgets constant. The authors should re-run the scaling experiment with fixed epoch counts and report the precise computational cost per model. This adjustment would prevent readers from conflating training protocol advantages with genuine architectural benefits.

- The assertion in Section 1 that large-scale training overrides inductive bias rests on a narrow set of benchmarks: The paper claims that massive datasets trump the locality and translation equivariance baked into CNNs, but the experiments focus almost exclusively on ImageNet and VTAB classification. Tasks requiring fine-grained spatial reasoning, small-object detection, or dense prediction heavily depend on those very inductive biases. The authors do not test ViT on datasets where spatial structure is known to be critical, leaving the claim partially speculative. Restricting the statement to mid-sized classification tasks and adding experiments on spatially complex benchmarks would ground the assertion in evidence. This expansion is necessary to accurately position ViT within the broader computer vision landscape.

- The masked patch prediction results in Section 4.6 trail supervised pre-training by four percentage points: Section 4.6 presents a preliminary self-supervised experiment that underperforms supervised training and then defers contrastive methods to future work. This leaves a critical question unanswered: why does masked modeling struggle with a flat patch sequence, and does it scale similarly to supervised objectives? The paper needs to analyze whether the lack of hierarchical structure impedes reconstruction, and it should compare masked modeling against a supervised baseline trained on the exact same data and compute budget. Without this analysis, the self-supervised section reads as an afterthought rather than a substantive investigation. Filling this gap would clarify the practical limits of self-supervised ViT training.

- Handling variable resolutions through 2D interpolation in Section 3.2 introduces unverified spatial distortions: Section 3.2 relies on interpolating pre-trained 1D position embeddings to handle higher-resolution fine-tuning, while claiming in Appendix D.3 that 2D-aware embeddings yield no significant gains. This claim rests on an unreported appendix and ignores how interpolation artifacts might distort spatial relationships at unseen resolutions. The paper should provide a controlled ablation comparing fixed 1D, interpolated 1D, and learned 2D position embeddings across multiple resolution shifts. Transparently reporting these results would strengthen the architectural justification and clarify limits for variable-resolution deployment. Readers need concrete evidence before accepting that interpolation preserves the necessary geometric structure.

- Synthetic demonstration of data requirements for attention vs convolution: The central claim that large-scale training overrides the lack of CNN inductive biases rests entirely on empirical curves across ImageNet and JFT-300M. Readers will wonder why the transition from data-hungry to data-efficient happens at these specific scales. A worked example on a synthetic dataset would ground this intuition. Specifically, generating a simple synthetic task that requires learning global correlations versus local spatial patterns would clearly show where ViT's performance plateaus and how it scales with dataset size compared to a convolutional baseline. This would isolate the architectural effect from dataset complexity and provide a concrete mechanism for the observed scaling behavior.

- Missing practical guidance on patch size selection and sequence length trade-offs: The paper demonstrates that smaller patch sizes improve accuracy but drastically increase sequence length and computational cost. It reports results for patch sizes of 14, 16, and 32 pixels but offers no framework for selecting the optimal patch size for a given compute budget or target resolution. Practitioners need clear guidance on how to balance the quadratic attention complexity against representational capacity when deploying the model. The authors should provide a decision rule or ablation study mapping patch size directly to FLOPs and accuracy gains across different input resolutions. This missing implementation discussion leaves a practical gap between the reported benchmarks and real-world deployment.

- Underdeveloped analysis of performance drops on specialized and geometric tasks: Table 2 and Figure 2 reveal a consistent performance drop on the Specialized and Structured VTAB task groups compared to Natural tasks. The paper notes this observation but stops short of analyzing why the architecture struggles with domain-specific or geometric reasoning. The lack of spatial inductive biases likely interacts poorly with tasks requiring precise localization or domain-shifted feature distributions. Developing this implication would clarify the boundaries of ViT's transfer learning capabilities and guide future architectural modifications for these domains. Without this analysis, the broad claim of matching state-of-the-art across diverse benchmarks remains partially unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Re-evaluate all baselines in Table 2 to ensure fair comparison with semi-supervised methods or explicitly isolate supervised performance from semi-supervised signals.

- Add a systematic ablation on data augmentation strategies and regularization techniques for both pre-training and fine-tuning to clarify their impact on ViT stability.

- Normalize the scaling study by fixed epoch counts and report exact FLOPs per model to decouple architectural efficiency from training duration and optimizer differences.

- Expand the self-supervised section with a detailed analysis of masked modeling limitations and a compute-matched comparison to supervised training.

- Provide a controlled ablation on positional encoding schemes across resolution shifts to validate the interpolation claim and document spatial distortion effects.

comments.title: Factual inaccuracy regarding outperformance on all tasks
comments.quote: The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: Table 2 directly contradicts this statement. On VTAB, BiT-L scores 76.29 ± 1.70 compared to ViT-L/16's 76.28 ± 0.46, meaning BiT-L slightly outperforms ViT-L/16. Additionally, on ImageNet Real, both models score exactly 90.54, resulting in a tie rather than an outperformance. The authors should revise this claim to accurately reflect that ViT-L/16 matches or slightly exceeds BiT-L on most tasks, with a marginal difference on VTAB.

comments.title: Table caption mischaracterizes baseline architectures
comments.quote: Vision Transformer models pre-trained on the JFT-300M dataset outperform ResNet-based baselines on all datasets, while taking substantially less computational resources to pre-train.
comments.explanation: This caption makes two inaccurate generalizations. First, as noted above, ViT-L/16 ties BiT-L on ImageNet Real and scores slightly lower on VTAB, so 'outperform on all datasets' is incorrect. Second, the caption refers to ResNet-based baselines, but the table includes Noisy Student, which is an EfficientNet, not a ResNet. The caption should be updated to specify CNN-based baselines and qualify the performance claim to match the actual results.

comments.title: Conceptual mismatch between linear probing and overfitting claims
comments.quote: Vision Transformers overfit more than ResNets with comparable computational cost on smaller datasets.
comments.explanation: The authors use few-shot linear probing accuracy to claim that ViTs overfit more than ResNets on smaller datasets. Linear probing evaluates the quality of frozen backbone features on a downstream task, which reflects representation learning capacity and generalization, not overfitting per se. Overfitting is typically measured by the training-validation accuracy gap or by full finetuning performance degradation. A lower linear probing score on small datasets indicates that the model fails to learn robust features, which could stem from insufficient data for the model's capacity rather than memorization. Rewrite the claim to state that ViTs learn inferior representations on limited data rather than overfitting, because linear probing measures feature quality and generalization capacity, not the training-validation accuracy gap that defines overfitting.

comments.title: Model counts conflate training runs with distinct architectures
comments.quote: The model set includes: 7 ResNets, R50x1, R50x2 R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus R152x2 and R200x3 pre-trained for 14 epochs; 6 Vision Transformers, ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus L/16 and H/14 pre-trained for 14 epochs; and 5 hybrids, R50+ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16 pretrained for 7 epochs, plus R50+ViT-L/16 pre-trained for 14 epochs
comments.explanation: The numbers 7, 6, and 5 count distinct training configurations rather than unique model architectures. Models like R152x2, ViT-L/16, and R50+ViT-L/16 are each evaluated at two different epoch counts (7 and 14), meaning the study actually assesses 6 distinct ResNets, 5 distinct ViTs, and 4 distinct hybrids. Listing the counts this way risks misleading readers into thinking 18 separate architectures were benchmarked. Rewrite the introductory sentence to specify that the counts refer to the total number of training runs evaluated across different epoch schedules.

comments.title: Hybrid naming explanation contradicts listed model suffixes
comments.quote: (for hybrids, the number at the end of the model name stands not for the patch size, but for the total dowsampling ratio in the ResNet backbone).
comments.explanation: This parenthetical note is factually inconsistent with the hybrid models listed. The ResNet-50 backbone uniformly downsamples the input by 32×, yet the list includes hybrids with suffixes like /16 and /32. The number in the hybrid name clearly denotes the Vision Transformer's patch size, not the ResNet's downsampling ratio. Additionally, dowsampling contains a typographical error. Clarify that the suffix indicates the ViT patch size, and correct the spelling to downsampling.

comments.title: Misclassification of ImageNet as a mid-sized or small benchmark
comments.quote: When pre-trained on large amounts of data and transferred to multiple mid-sized or small image recognition benchmarks (ImageNet, CIFAR-100, VTAB, etc.), Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: The abstract explicitly categorizes ImageNet as a 'mid-sized or small' benchmark alongside CIFAR-100 and VTAB. This classification contradicts standard computer vision terminology, where ImageNet-1K is universally treated as a large-scale dataset, particularly when contrasted with truly small datasets like CIFAR or the specialized VTAB tasks. The paper's own scaling analysis later treats ImageNet as a substantial benchmark that benefits from massive pre-training data, making this descriptor internally inconsistent. Readers may question the authors' framing of dataset scales or assume a non-standard dataset split was used. Rewrite the parenthetical list as '(e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-100, and VTAB)' or change the descriptor to 'standard and specialized image recognition benchmarks' to align with field conventions and the paper's internal scaling narrative.

comments.title: Misattributed citation for 100B parameter models
comments.quote: Thanks to Transformers’ computational efficiency and scalability, it has become possible to train models of unprecedented size, with over 100B parameters *(Brown et al., 2020; Lepikhin et al., 2020)*.
comments.explanation: The citation to Lepikhin et al. (2020) is inaccurate for the 100B parameter claim. That paper introduces BigBird, which contains 1.6B parameters and focuses on efficient attention for long sequences, not massive model scaling. The 100B+ parameter milestone is primarily associated with GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) and later mixture-of-experts models like Switch Transformer (Fedus et al., 2021). Remove the Lepikhin citation from this specific claim or replace it with a reference that actually discusses models of that scale.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of BERT's pre-training objective
comments.quote: BERT *(Devlin et al., 2019)* uses a denoising self-supervised pre-training task
comments.explanation: BERT's pre-training objective is specifically Masked Language Modeling (MLM), where random subwords are replaced with a [MASK] token and the model predicts the original tokens based on bidirectional context. While MLM shares conceptual similarities with denoising, it is distinct from Denoising Autoencoders (DAE) or diffusion-based approaches that typically reconstruct inputs from corrupted continuous signals or add Gaussian noise. Using the term 'denoising' here conflates BERT's discrete token masking with continuous signal reconstruction methods, which could confuse readers familiar with the specific terminology in NLP and self-supervised learning literature.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of iGPT's architecture and training
comments.quote: Another recent related model is image GPT (iGPT) *(Chen et al., 2020a)*, which applies Transformers to image pixels after reducing image resolution and color space.
comments.explanation: The description of iGPT omits its core mechanism. iGPT does not apply Transformers directly to continuous pixel values after simple resolution or color reduction. Instead, it employs a Vector Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) to discretize images into a sequence of tokens from a learned codebook, and then trains an autoregressive Transformer to predict these discrete tokens. Omitting the VQ-VAE component misrepresents the model's architecture and its self-supervised learning objective, which relies on discrete token prediction rather than continuous pixel regression or classification.

comments.title: Misleading claim of architectural similarity to original Transformer
comments.quote: In model design we follow the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) as closely as possible. An advantage of this intentionally simple setup is that scalable NLP Transformer architectures – and their efficient implementations – can be used almost out of the box.
comments.explanation: The claim that the model follows the original Transformer 'as closely as possible' is methodologically imprecise and obscures critical architectural adaptations required for image data. The original Transformer relies on sinusoidal positional encodings and processes sequential tokens without a dedicated classification token, whereas ViT introduces learned 2D positional embeddings and a [CLS] token specifically for image classification. Additionally, the patch embedding layer fundamentally alters the input representation compared to the word embeddings in the original architecture. Stating this without acknowledging these necessary deviations creates ambiguity about the actual model design and complicates reproducibility for readers expecting a direct port of the NLP architecture. Rewrite the quoted text as 'In model design we adapt the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) by introducing learned 2D positional embeddings, a [CLS] token, and a patch embedding layer, while retaining the core attention and feed-forward structure.' because claiming a near-identical port to the NLP architecture obscures the actual design and complicates reproducibility.

comments.title: Sequence length notation conflicts with class token addition
comments.quote: $N = HW / P^2$  is the resulting number of patches, which also serves as the effective input sequence length for the Transformer.
comments.explanation: The text claims that $N$ serves as the effective input sequence length, but the Transformer actually processes $N+1$ tokens (one class token plus $N$ patches). This is explicitly reflected in Equation (1), where the position embedding matrix $ oldsymbol{E}_{pos}$ has shape $(N+1) 	imes D$. The condition appears too strong because it ignores the added token. Rewrite '$N = HW / P^2$ is the resulting number of patches, which also serves as the effective input sequence length for the Transformer' as '$N = HW / P^2$ is the resulting number of patches, yielding an effective input sequence length of $N+1$ for the Transformer' because the class token increases the sequence length by one.

comments.title: Non-standard target encoding for linear probing
comments.quote: taset. Few-shot accuracies are obtained by solving a regularized least-squares regression problem that maps the (frozen) representation of a subset of training images to  $\{-1,1\}^K$  target vectors.
comments.explanation: The target space $ oldsymbol{-1,1}^K$ is non-standard for multi-class classification linear probing, which typically uses one-hot encoded vectors in $ oldsymbol{0,1}^K$. Using $ oldsymbol{-1,1}$ for K classes creates ambiguity in the least-squares formulation unless a specific binary coding scheme is intended. This appears to be a typo for $ oldsymbol{0,1}^K$. Rewrite the target space as $ oldsymbol{0,1}^K$ to align with standard linear probing practices and ensure the closed-form solution matches conventional implementations.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of self-supervised performance gap
comments.quote: Our initial experiments show improvement from self-supervised pre-training, but there is still large gap between self-supervised and large-scale supervised pretraining.
comments.explanation: The conclusion broadly attributes the performance gap to 'self-supervised pre-training' as a category, but Section 4.6 only evaluates masked patch prediction. The authors explicitly note that contrastive self-supervised methods are left for future work. Because contrastive approaches often close this gap significantly, the current phrasing may mislead readers into thinking all self-supervised paradigms underperform supervised pre-training by a similar margin. To accurately reflect the technical results, the claim should be scoped to the specific method tested. Rewrite 'Our initial experiments show improvement from self-supervised pre-training, but there is still large gap between self-supervised and large-scale supervised pretraining.' as 'Our initial experiments with masked patch prediction show improvement over random initialization, but there is still a large gap between this reconstruction-based approach and large-scale supervised pretraining.' because this aligns the concluding summary with the narrow experimental scope documented in Section 4.6.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper demonstrates that a standard Transformer architecture, when applied to image patches and pre-trained on massive datasets, can achieve state-of-the-art results on image classification, effectively challenging the long-standing dominance of convolutional neural networks (CNNs).

The paper provides a compelling and well-executed empirical study that shifts the paradigm of computer vision architectures. Its strength lies in the clear demonstration that large-scale data can compensate for the lack of traditional inductive biases, providing a new, scalable foundation for vision models.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity to Pre-training Scale: The paper acknowledges that ViT underperforms compared to ResNets when trained on smaller datasets like ImageNet, yet the implications for practical, real-world deployment are not fully explored. The reliance on JFT-300M or ImageNet-21k for competitive performance limits the accessibility of this architecture for researchers or practitioners without massive compute resources. The authors should provide a more detailed analysis or guidelines on the 'crossover point' where ViT becomes superior to CNNs, specifically in terms of dataset size and model parameter count, to help readers understand when this architecture is actually appropriate for their specific constraints.

- Lack of Inductive Bias and Data Efficiency: While the authors correctly identify that ViT lacks the locality and translation equivariance of CNNs, the paper does not sufficiently address how this impacts the model's ability to generalize on fine-grained or low-data tasks. Section 4.3 touches on this with few-shot evaluation, but the discussion remains brief. It would be valuable to include a more rigorous analysis of how the model performs on tasks where spatial structure is paramount, such as object detection or segmentation, rather than just classification. This would clarify whether the global attention mechanism truly compensates for the loss of local inductive biases in tasks beyond simple category labeling.

- Computational and Memory Overhead: The paper highlights the efficiency of the Transformer encoder, but the quadratic complexity of self-attention with respect to sequence length (patch count) is a significant bottleneck for high-resolution imagery. While the authors use 2D interpolation for positional embeddings during fine-tuning, the memory footprint of the attention matrix remains a concern for larger input sizes. A more explicit discussion of the scalability limits of the current patch-based approach, perhaps comparing the memory usage of ViT versus modern efficient CNNs at varying resolutions, would strengthen the claims regarding computational efficiency.

- Interpretability of Attention Mechanisms: Section 4.5 provides a qualitative look at attention maps, but the analysis is largely descriptive. The claim that the model attends to semantically relevant regions is intuitive, yet the paper lacks a quantitative assessment of how these attention patterns correlate with model performance or robustness. Adding a metric to evaluate the 'focus' or 'sparsity' of attention across different layers could provide a more objective understanding of how the model learns to prioritize spatial information compared to the fixed receptive fields of convolutional layers.

- Lack of explicit crossover point analysis for data regimes: The paper argues that large-scale training compensates for the lack of inductive bias, yet it fails to provide a formal characterization of the data-regime crossover point. Readers need a clear decision rule or a computed threshold—such as the number of training samples per class required for ViT-Base to match a ResNet-50—to understand when the architectural shift is justified. I suggest adding a plot or table that explicitly maps the 'crossover' dataset size against model depth/width, using a standard benchmark like ImageNet-1k as the baseline, to provide actionable guidance for practitioners.

- Missing quantitative evaluation of attention sparsity and focus: While the paper provides qualitative attention maps, it stops short of quantifying the 'focus' of the self-attention mechanism. To support the claim that ViT learns spatial relationships comparable to convolutional receptive fields, the authors should compute the average attention entropy or spatial dispersion metric across layers. A comparison of this metric between ViT and a standard ResNet (using Grad-CAM or similar) would provide the necessary evidence that the global attention mechanism is effectively learning local structure.

- Absence of memory-scaling benchmarks for high-resolution inputs: The paper claims the architecture is scalable, but ignores the practical memory bottleneck imposed by the quadratic complexity of global self-attention as resolution increases. A concrete computation of the memory footprint (FLOPs and peak VRAM usage) for ViT-L/16 versus a comparable ResNet at resolutions beyond 512x512 is necessary to validate the efficiency claims. This would clarify the 'scaling limits' mentioned in the conclusion and provide a realistic view of the hardware requirements for high-resolution downstream tasks.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a more comprehensive analysis of the performance crossover point between ViT and CNNs relative to dataset size and compute budget.

- Expand the discussion on the limitations of the current patch-based approach for high-resolution inputs and dense prediction tasks.

- Provide a quantitative assessment of attention map patterns to move beyond purely qualitative visualizations.

comments.title: Incorrect claim regarding MLP translational equivariance
comments.quote: In ViT, only MLP layers are local and translationally equivariant, while the self-attention layers are global.
comments.explanation: The assertion that MLP layers in the Vision Transformer are translationally equivariant is incorrect. These layers consist of point-wise (1x1) fully connected operations applied to each patch embedding independently. While the weights are shared across patches, this does not constitute spatial translational equivariance in the sense of a convolutional layer, which preserves and exploits 2D neighborhood structure. Please revise this to state that the MLP layers are applied position-wise.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'out of the box' Transformer usage
comments.quote: An advantage of this intentionally simple setup is that scalable NLP Transformer architectures – and their efficient implementations – can be used almost out of the box.
comments.explanation: The claim that NLP Transformer architectures can be used 'out of the box' for images is misleading. The original Transformer operates on discrete tokens, whereas images are continuous 2D signals. The methodology requires a critical patch-embedding projection to map 2D patches into the 1D sequence format. Please clarify that this 'out of the box' claim refers specifically to the Transformer encoder block, not the entire model architecture, by explicitly mentioning the patch-to-token projection layer.

comments.title: Inconsistency in hybrid model naming convention
comments.quote: (for hybrids, the number at the end of the model name stands not for the patch size, but for the total dowsampling ratio in the ResNet backbone).
comments.explanation: The text states that the number at the end of the hybrid model name (e.g., R50+ViT-B/32) represents the total downsampling ratio of the ResNet backbone. However, standard ResNet-50 architectures have a total downsampling ratio of 32, which creates ambiguity with the patch size convention used for non-hybrid models. Please clarify whether this number refers to the patch size of the transformer component or the stride of the backbone, as the current explanation is confusing.

comments.title: Ambiguity in classification head definition
comments.quote:
Both during pre-training and fine-tuning, a classification head is attached to  $\mathbf{z}_L^0$ . The classification head is implemented by a MLP with one hidden layer at pre-training time and by a single linear layer at fine-tuning time.

Position
comments.explanation: Equation 4 defines the final representation y as the LayerNorm-normalized output of the class token, which remains in the latent dimension D. However, the text describes the classification head as a linear layer during fine-tuning, which would map from D to the number of classes K. Equation 4 should be interpreted as the final feature representation before the classification head. Please rewrite Equation 4 to define the feature representation and add a subsequent line defining the classification head mapping to K classes.

comments.title: Missing formal definition of attention distance
comments.quote: Specifically, we compute the average distance in image space across which information is integrated, based on the attention weights (Figure 7, right). This "attention distance" is analogous to receptive field size in CNNs.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'attention distance' as a quantitative metric but omit the formal definition of the calculation. Without specifying whether this is a weighted Euclidean distance or another metric, the comparison to CNN receptive fields is ambiguous. Please provide the formula, such as d = sum(w_ij * ||p_i - p_j||_2), where w_ij are the attention weights and p_i, p_j are the patch coordinates.

comments.title: Citation year discrepancy for FixRes methodology
comments.quote: s often beneficial to fine-tune at higher resolution than pre-training *(Touvron et al., 2019; Kolesnikov et al., 2020)*. When
comments.explanation: The citation of Touvron et al. (2019) refers to an arXiv preprint. The peer-reviewed version, which formally established the FixRes methodology, was published in the proceedings of the 2020 NeurIPS conference. Please replace 'Touvron et al., 2019' with 'Touvron et al., 2020' to align with the finalized publication record.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces the Vision Transformer (ViT), a pure transformer architecture applied directly to image patches, and demonstrates that with large-scale pre-training it matches or exceeds state-of-the-art convolutional networks on several image classification benchmarks. The work is significant for showing that convolutional inductive biases are not strictly necessary for strong visual recognition performance, provided sufficient data is available. The experiments are carefully controlled, the scaling analysis is informative, and the paper is clearly written.

Overview issues:

- Data requirements for good performance are not rigorously quantified: The paper claims that ViT outperforms CNNs when pre-trained on large datasets (JFT-300M), but the analysis of pre-training data requirements is incomplete. Figure 4 shows few-shot accuracy on ImageNet for random subsets of JFT, but this does not directly measure fine-tuning performance, which is the primary evaluation metric. Moreover, the paper does not systematically vary dataset size while controlling for model capacity and compute budget, making it hard to isolate the effect of data scale. The authors should provide a more thorough ablation that reports fine-tuning accuracy (not just few-shot) across multiple dataset sizes and model variants, and should discuss the minimum dataset size at which ViT becomes competitive with CNNs.

- Comparison to convolutional baselines does not fully control for compute budget: While Table 2 reports TPUv3-core-days for pre-training, the comparison is not entirely fair because the ViT models use different training schedules, optimizers, and regularization than the baselines. The paper acknowledges this in Section 4.2 but does not provide a controlled experiment where all models are trained with the same hyperparameters and compute budget. The scaling study in Section 4.4 partially addresses this, but it only evaluates transfer performance from JFT-300M, not from smaller datasets. A more rigorous comparison would include experiments where ResNets and ViTs are trained with identical training protocols (same optimizer, learning rate schedule, weight decay, etc.) across multiple dataset sizes.

- Ablation studies on key design choices are missing or insufficient: The paper makes several critical design decisions—patch size, positional encoding type, and the use of a classification token—but does not provide systematic ablation studies to justify them. Appendix D.3 claims that 2D-aware position embeddings do not improve performance, but the details are sparse and the experiment is not described in the main text. Similarly, the choice of patch size (16x16 or 14x14) is not ablated; the paper only presents results for a few fixed sizes. The authors should include a dedicated ablation section that varies patch size, positional encoding (1D vs. 2D vs. none), and the classification head design (CLS token vs. global pooling), and report the impact on both pre-training and fine-tuning performance.

- Self-supervised pre-training comparison is superficial and incomplete: Section 4.6 reports a single self-supervised experiment (masked patch prediction) that achieves 79.9% on ImageNet, which is 4% below supervised pre-training. However, the paper does not compare to state-of-the-art self-supervised methods for CNNs (e.g., SimCLR, MoCo) under controlled conditions. The experiment uses only ViT-B/16 and does not explore larger models or different self-supervised objectives (e.g., contrastive learning). Given that self-supervision is a major motivation for transformers in NLP, this analysis is too limited. The authors should either expand this section with more comprehensive experiments or clearly state that self-supervised ViT is not yet competitive and defer a full study to future work.

- Generalization to other vision domains is not demonstrated: The paper evaluates ViT on standard classification benchmarks (ImageNet, CIFAR, VTAB) but does not test on tasks like object detection, segmentation, or video recognition, which are important for establishing ViT as a general-purpose vision backbone. The conclusion mentions this as future work, but the paper's title and framing imply that ViT is a general solution for image recognition. Without experiments on dense prediction tasks, it is unclear whether the approach generalizes beyond classification. The authors should either include preliminary results on detection or segmentation (e.g., using ViT as a backbone in a standard framework like DETR or Mask R-CNN) or clearly delimit the scope of the paper to classification.

- Lack of analysis on the impact of fine-tuning resolution and strategy: The paper fine-tunes ViT at higher resolutions than pre-training (e.g., 512 for ViT-L/16) and uses 2D interpolation of position embeddings. However, there is no systematic study of how fine-tuning resolution affects performance, nor a comparison of different fine-tuning strategies (e.g., linear probing vs. full fine-tuning, different learning rates, head designs). The authors should provide an ablation that varies fine-tuning resolution and reports the trade-off between accuracy and compute, and should discuss whether the interpolation of position embeddings introduces artifacts or limits performance.

- Missing controlled comparison on small datasets: The paper claims that ViT underperforms on mid-sized datasets like ImageNet, but the only evidence is a brief mention in the introduction and Figure 3, which shows ViT-Large under ViT-Base when pre-trained on ImageNet. However, there is no systematic experiment that varies regularization strength (weight decay, dropout, label smoothing) for each model size on a fixed small dataset, nor a comparison to a ResNet trained with the same protocol. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the gap is fundamental or merely due to suboptimal hyperparameters. The authors should add an ablation on ImageNet (1.3M images) that sweeps key regularization parameters for ViT-B, ViT-L, and a ResNet-50, reporting fine-tuning accuracy after pre-training on ImageNet only.

- No analysis of patch size's effect on performance: The paper uses patch sizes of 16×16 and 14×14 for most experiments, but never systematically varies patch size while controlling for model capacity and compute. Patch size directly determines sequence length and thus the computational cost and the granularity of spatial information available to the Transformer. Without an ablation showing accuracy vs. patch size (e.g., 8×8, 16×16, 32×32) for a fixed model variant (say ViT-B), the claim that the design is 'simple' and 'direct' lacks empirical support. The authors should add a table or figure reporting ImageNet fine-tuning accuracy and pre-training cost for ViT-B with patch sizes 8, 16, and 32, pre-trained on ImageNet-21k.

- Missing discussion of estimation of position embeddings for new resolutions: The paper fine-tunes at higher resolutions by interpolating pre-trained position embeddings (Section 3.2), but provides no guidance on how to perform this interpolation (e.g., bilinear vs. nearest-neighbor, handling of the class token embedding) and no analysis of how the interpolation quality affects downstream accuracy. For a practitioner wanting to apply ViT to a custom resolution, this is a critical implementation detail. The authors should include a brief description of the interpolation method used (e.g., 'we use bicubic interpolation on the 2D grid of position embeddings, excluding the class token') and report an ablation on ImageNet where fine-tuning resolution is varied from 224 to 640, showing the effect on accuracy and training time.

- No comparison to Cordonnier et al. under controlled conditions: The related work section mentions Cordonnier et al. (2020) as the most related model, but the paper never compares ViT to that approach experimentally. The claim that ViT goes further by demonstrating large-scale pre-training effectiveness would be stronger if the authors showed that Cordonnier's model does not benefit from the same scaling. The authors should pre-train a model following Cordonnier's design (2×2 patches, small resolution) on JFT-300M or ImageNet-21k and report its ImageNet fine-tuning accuracy, or explain why such a comparison is infeasible (e.g., the model cannot handle 224×224 images due to sequence length).

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a controlled ablation study that systematically varies patch size, positional encoding type, and the use of a classification token, reporting their impact on both pre-training and fine-tuning performance.

- Expand the analysis of pre-training data requirements by reporting fine-tuning accuracy (not just few-shot) across multiple dataset sizes (e.g., 9M, 30M, 90M, 300M) for several model sizes, and discuss the minimum data scale needed for ViT to outperform CNNs.

- Include a more rigorous comparison to convolutional baselines where training hyperparameters (optimizer, schedule, weight decay) are matched across architectures, and report performance-compute Pareto frontiers for multiple dataset sizes.

- Either add preliminary results on object detection or segmentation using ViT as a backbone, or clearly state in the title and abstract that the paper focuses on image classification only.

- Conduct a systematic study of fine-tuning resolution and strategy, including an ablation of different resolutions and a comparison of linear probing vs. full fine-tuning, and discuss the impact of position embedding interpolation.

comments.title: Figure 7 is missing from the manuscript
comments.quote: Figure 7 (left) shows the top principal components of the learned embedding filters. The components resemble plausible basis functions for a low-dimensional representation of the fine structure within each patch.
comments.explanation: The section repeatedly refers to Figure 7 (left, center, right) but the figure itself is not included in the provided text. The only figure present is Figure 6. Without Figure 7, the claims about principal components, position embedding similarity, and attention distance are unverifiable. The authors must ensure Figure 7 is included in the final manuscript. This is a missing artifact, not a mathematical error, but it blocks evaluation of the section's core claims.

comments.title: Attention distance metric is not defined
comments.quote: Specifically, we compute the average distance in image space across which information is integrated, based on the attention weights (Figure 7, right). This "attention distance" is analogous to receptive field size in CNNs.
comments.explanation: The term "attention distance" is introduced without a formal definition. The reader cannot verify how the average distance is computed from the attention weights — for example, whether it is a weighted average over all patch pairs, a median, or something else. Without this definition, the claim that some heads "attend to most of the image already in the lowest layers" is ambiguous. Add a precise formula for attention distance, e.g., as the expected spatial distance between the query patch and the key patches weighted by the attention probabilities. This is essential for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent model counts in scaling study
comments.quote: The model set includes: 7 ResNets, R50x1, R50x2 R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus R152x2 and R200x3 pre-trained for 14 epochs; 6 Vision Transformers, ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus L/16 and H/14 pre-trained for 14 epochs; and 5 hybrids, R50+ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16 pretrained for 7 epochs, plus R50+ViT-L/16 pre-trained for 14 epochs
comments.explanation: The text claims 7 ResNets but only lists 6 distinct architectures (R50x1, R50x2, R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, R200x3). The count of 7 appears to double-count R152x2 because it appears in both the 7-epoch and 14-epoch groups. Similarly, for Vision Transformers, the text claims 6 models but lists 5 distinct architectures (ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, H/14), with L/16 appearing in both epoch groups. The hybrid count of 5 lists 4 distinct architectures (R50+ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16), with R50+ViT-L/16 added for 14 epochs. The authors should clarify whether they are counting distinct architectures or architecture-epoch combinations, and correct the numbers to match the listed items.

comments.title: Self-supervised result lacks critical context
comments.quote: With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves  $79.9\%$  accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of  $2\%$  to training from scratch, but still  $4\%$  behind supervised pre-training.
comments.explanation: The claim that 79.9% is a 'significant improvement of 2% to training from scratch' is ambiguous and potentially misleading. The paper does not report the 'training from scratch' baseline accuracy for ViT-B/16 on ImageNet in this section, so the reader cannot verify the 2% improvement. Moreover, the 4% gap to supervised pre-training is stated without specifying which supervised pre-training baseline is used (e.g., pre-training on ImageNet-21k or JFT-300M). Without these baselines, the comparison is uninformative. The authors should explicitly state the 'training from scratch' accuracy and the supervised pre-training accuracy for the same model (ViT-B/16) in the same sentence, or remove the quantitative comparisons if the baselines are not defined here.

comments.title: Claim of matching or exceeding state of the art is overstated
comments.quote: Thus, Vision Transformer matches or exceeds the state of the art on many image classification datasets, whilst being relatively cheap to pre-train.
comments.explanation: The paper's own results show that ViT only matches or exceeds state-of-the-art CNNs when pre-trained on the largest dataset (JFT-300M). On ImageNet (1.3M images) and other mid-sized datasets, ViT underperforms comparable ResNets. The quoted claim does not qualify this dependence on pre-training data scale, which is a central finding of the paper. The conclusion should explicitly state the data scale condition under which ViT achieves competitive performance. Rewrite as: 'Thus, when pre-trained on sufficiently large datasets (e.g., JFT-300M), Vision Transformer matches or exceeds the state of the art on many image classification benchmarks, while being relatively cheap to pre-train.'

comments.title: Claim of cheap pre-training is not quantified
comments.quote: whilst being relatively cheap to pre-train.
comments.explanation: The paper reports pre-training costs in TPUv3-core-days (e.g., ViT-L/16 costs 2.5k TPUv3-core-days), but does not compare these costs to those of the state-of-the-art CNNs it claims to match or exceed. Without such a comparison, the claim of 'relatively cheap' is unsupported. The conclusion should either provide a direct cost comparison or remove the claim. Remove 'whilst being relatively cheap to pre-train' because the paper does not establish that ViT is cheaper than comparable CNNs under controlled conditions.

comments.title: Hybrid naming convention contradicts earlier definition
comments.quote: for hybrids, the number at the end of the model name stands not for the patch size, but for the total dowsampling ratio in the ResNet backbone
comments.explanation: The text states that for hybrids, the trailing number indicates total downsampling ratio, not patch size. However, the listed hybrids include 'R50+ViT-B/32' and 'R50+ViT-L/16', where the numbers 32 and 16 are part of the ViT component name, not the hybrid name. This creates confusion: does the hybrid name inherit the ViT patch size or the ResNet downsampling ratio? The paper should explicitly define the hybrid naming convention and ensure consistency between the listed names and the explanation.

comments.title: Claim about hybrid models lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: This highly localized attention is less pronounced in hybrid models that apply a ResNet before the Transformer (Figure 7, right), suggesting that it may serve a similar function as early convolutional layers in CNNs.
comments.explanation: The statement that 'highly localized attention is less pronounced in hybrid models' is qualitative and relies on visual inspection of Figure 7, which is not provided. No numerical comparison (e.g., average attention distance at each layer for ViT vs. hybrid) is given. The reader cannot assess whether the difference is significant or merely anecdotal. The authors should report the mean attention distance per layer for both model types, perhaps in a table or as a numerical annotation on the figure.

comments.title: Self-supervised gap claim lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: Our initial experiments show improvement from self-supervised pre-training, but there is still large gap between self-supervised and large-scale supervised pretraining.
comments.explanation: The paper reports only one self-supervised experiment (masked patch prediction) achieving 79.9% on ImageNet, which is about 4% below supervised pre-training. The phrase 'large gap' is vague and not quantified. Moreover, the paper does not compare to state-of-the-art self-supervised methods for CNNs (e.g., SimCLR, MoCo), so the reader cannot assess whether this gap is specific to ViT or reflects a general limitation of self-supervised methods at the time. The conclusion should either state the exact gap (e.g., '4 percentage points on ImageNet') or acknowledge that the comparison is limited to a single method. Rewrite as: 'Our initial experiments with masked patch prediction show improvement from self-supervised pre-training, but there remains a gap of approximately 4 percentage points on ImageNet compared to large-scale supervised pre-training. A more comprehensive comparison to other self-supervised approaches is needed.'

comments.title: Missing comma in ResNet list causes ambiguity
comments.quote: 7 ResNets, R50x1, R50x2 R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, pre-trained for 7 epochs
comments.explanation: There is a missing comma between 'R50x2' and 'R101x1', which could be misread as a single model 'R50x2 R101x1'. The intended list is likely 'R50x1, R50x2, R101x1, R152x1, R152x2'. Add a comma after 'R50x2' for clarity.

comments.title: Position embedding analysis conflates similarity with structure
comments.quote: Figure 7 (center) shows that the model learns to encode distance within the image in the similarity of position embeddings, i.e. closer patches tend to have more similar position embeddings. Further, the row-column structure appears; patches in the same row/column have similar embeddings.
comments.explanation: The text claims that 'closer patches tend to have more similar position embeddings' and that 'row-column structure appears.' However, it does not quantify this similarity (e.g., by computing the correlation between spatial distance and cosine similarity of position embeddings). Without a quantitative measure, the claim is subjective. Moreover, the observation that row-column structure appears is expected from any 2D positional encoding that respects spatial locality; the novelty would be if ViT learns a non-trivial structure beyond simple distance decay. The authors should provide a correlation coefficient or a plot of similarity vs. distance to substantiate the claim.

comments.title: Missing comparison to supervised pre-training baselines
comments.quote: With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves  $79.9\%$  accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of  $2\%$  to training from scratch, but still  $4\%$  behind supervised pre-training.
comments.explanation: The sentence compares the self-supervised result to 'supervised pre-training' without specifying which supervised pre-training dataset or model is used. From the paper's main results, ViT-B/16 achieves 79.9% with self-supervision, but supervised pre-training on ImageNet-21k yields 85.1% (from Table 2). The 4% gap is relative to an unspecified baseline, and the reader must cross-reference other sections to understand the comparison. This is a clarity issue that could mislead readers into thinking the gap is smaller than it actually is. The authors should explicitly state the supervised pre-training baseline (e.g., 'supervised pre-training on ImageNet-21k achieves 84.0%') in this sentence to provide proper context.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Vision Transformers (ViT), a method that applies the Transformer architecture directly to image patches without convolutional inductive biases. The authors demonstrate that ViT achieves state-of-the-art results on image classification benchmarks when pre-trained on large datasets like JFT-300M, outperforming ResNets with lower computational cost. The work is methodologically sound and empirically strong, providing a clear scaling law for vision models.

The paper presents a significant empirical contribution by validating the scaling hypothesis in computer vision. The methodology is rigorous, with extensive ablation studies on data requirements and computational efficiency. The results are compelling and clearly communicated. While the paper focuses primarily on classification, the authors acknowledge the limitations regarding dense prediction tasks and data efficiency.

Overview issues:

- Data Efficiency and Inductive Bias Trade-off: The paper demonstrates that Vision Transformers (ViT) require significantly larger datasets (JFT-300M) to match or exceed the performance of ResNets (BiT) on smaller datasets like ImageNet. This finding, presented in Section 4.3, is a critical limitation. While the authors acknowledge this in the Introduction and Conclusion, the paper does not provide a concrete solution or a theoretical framework for addressing this data-hungry nature. The authors simply state that 'large scale training trumps inductive bias' (Section 1.2), but this is an empirical observation, not a general principle. The paper's contribution is limited to showing that ViT can be competitive at scale, but it fails to address the fundamental challenge of applying Transformers to vision tasks with limited data. A more complete analysis would include a comparison of the scaling laws for ViT and ResNets, or a discussion of how to inject inductive biases to reduce data requirements. The paper's framing as a 'general-purpose' vision architecture is therefore somewhat premature given this limitation.

- Computational Cost and Memory Constraints: The paper highlights that ViT has a quadratic computational complexity with respect to the number of patches, which is a direct consequence of the global self-attention mechanism. While the authors show that ViT is more efficient than ResNets in terms of pre-training compute for comparable performance (Section 4.4), this efficiency comes at the cost of high memory usage during training, especially for high-resolution images. The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of the memory footprint or a discussion of practical strategies for mitigating this issue. For example, the authors mention that 'ViT can handle arbitrary sequence lengths (up to memory constraints)' (Section 3.2), but they do not provide any concrete recommendations for handling high-resolution images or large batch sizes. This is a significant practical limitation that hinders the adoption of ViT for many real-world applications. The paper's claim that ViT is 'relatively cheap to pre-train' (Section 5) is therefore somewhat misleading, as it ignores the substantial memory requirements.

- Limited Scope of Tasks and Benchmarks: The paper focuses exclusively on image classification tasks. While the authors mention in the Conclusion that 'challenges remain' for other tasks like detection and segmentation, the paper does not provide any empirical evidence or analysis of ViT's performance on these tasks. This is a significant omission, as the ability to generalize to other vision tasks is a key criterion for a 'general-purpose' vision architecture. The paper's experimental setup is therefore incomplete. A more comprehensive evaluation would include a set of benchmarks for object detection, semantic segmentation, and other dense prediction tasks. The authors' claim that 'ViT matches or exceeds the state of the art on many image classification datasets' (Section 5) is therefore limited in scope. The paper's contribution is primarily to the field of image classification, not to the broader field of computer vision.

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for Scaling Laws: The paper provides extensive empirical evidence for the scaling laws of Vision Transformers, showing that performance improves with model size, dataset size, and compute. However, the paper does not provide a theoretical justification for these observations. The authors simply state that 'Vision Transformers appear not to saturate within the range tried' (Section 4.4), but they do not explain why this is the case. A more complete analysis would include a derivation of scaling laws based on information theory or statistical learning theory. This would provide a deeper understanding of the fundamental principles at play and guide future research. The paper's contribution is therefore largely empirical, lacking a strong theoretical foundation.

- Demonstration of non-vacuousity on low-data benchmarks: The paper claims that Vision Transformers (ViT) perform well when pre-trained on large datasets and transferred to tasks with fewer datapoints (Section 1.2). However, it does not provide a concrete example of a DGP (Data Generating Process) or a specific dataset where the ViT's lack of inductive biases causes it to fail or underperform relative to CNNs when data is scarce. The paper shows that ViT outperforms CNNs when pre-trained on JFT-300M (Section 4.2), but this is the regime where data is abundant. To demonstrate that the result has bite, the authors should compute the performance of ViT on a low-data transfer scenario (e.g., using only 1k or 10k images from ImageNet-21k) and compare it to a standard CNN (like ResNet-50). This would show that the lack of inductive biases is indeed a limitation in the low-data regime, which is a common scenario in practice.

- Worked special case: Low-resolution image processing: The paper introduces a hybrid architecture where the input sequence is formed from feature maps of a CNN (Section 3.2). This is presented as a special case of the general ViT framework. However, the authors do not provide a concrete, fully-specified example of this hybrid model. For instance, they should compute the performance of a ViT applied to the feature maps of a ResNet-50 at a specific stage (e.g., stage 3) and compare it to the standard ViT applied to raw image patches. This would allow readers to calibrate their intuitions about the trade-off between computational cost and performance in the hybrid setting.

- Underdeveloped implications for self-supervised pre-training: The paper mentions in the Conclusion that 'exploration of contrastive pre-training' is an exciting direction of future work (Section 4.6). However, the paper's abstract and introduction promise that ViT 'attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks' (Section 1.2). This claim is based on supervised pre-training. The paper does not discuss the implications of the ViT architecture for self-supervised pre-training methods. Specifically, it does not explain whether the lack of inductive biases in ViT makes it more or less suitable for self-supervised pre-training compared to CNNs. Developing this implication would require a more detailed analysis of the self-supervised pre-training results presented in Section 4.6.

- Missing comparison to existing approaches for low-data transfer: The paper compares ViT to state-of-the-art CNNs (BiT and Noisy Student) on standard benchmarks (Section 4.2). However, it does not formally compare the performance of ViT to these CNNs on the low-data transfer benchmarks (e.g., VTAB, ImageNet with 1000 training examples). The paper mentions that ViT performs well on VTAB (Section 4.2), but it does not provide a detailed comparison to the CNN baselines on this specific benchmark. This omission undermines the paper's claim that ViT is a 'general-purpose' vision architecture, as the low-data regime is a key test for generalization.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a more detailed analysis of the memory footprint and practical strategies for mitigating the quadratic complexity of ViT, especially for high-resolution images.

- Include a comprehensive evaluation of ViT on dense prediction tasks such as object detection and semantic segmentation to assess its generalization capabilities.

- Develop a theoretical framework or provide a deeper analysis of the scaling laws for ViT to justify the empirical observations and guide future research.

- Address the data efficiency limitation by exploring methods to inject inductive biases or by providing a more nuanced discussion of the trade-offs between inductive bias and data requirements.

comments.title: Abstract claims contradict paper-level context
comments.quote: Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that ViT requires 'substantially fewer computational resources to train,' but the paper-level context explicitly identifies this as a major limitation: the paper shows ViT has a quadratic computational complexity with respect to the number of patches and high memory usage during training, especially for high-resolution images. The paper-level context notes that 'ViT can handle arbitrary sequence lengths (up to memory constraints)' but does not provide concrete recommendations for handling high-resolution images or large batch sizes. The claim in the abstract is misleading given the detailed analysis in the paper-level context. Rewrite the abstract to remove the claim about requiring 'substantially fewer computational resources to train' and instead state that ViT 'attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks' without the computational resource claim, or qualify it with specific conditions (e.g., 'for comparable performance' or 'under specific scaling regimes').

comments.title: Inaccurate Description of iGPT Performance
comments.quote: The model is trained in an unsupervised fashion as a generative model, and the resulting representation can then be fine-tuned or probed linearly for classification performance, achieving a maximal accuracy of 72% on ImageNet.
comments.explanation: The reported accuracy of 72% for iGPT on ImageNet is incorrect. The original iGPT paper (Chen et al., 2020a) reported a linear probe accuracy of approximately 76.5% on ImageNet, and a fine-tuned accuracy of approximately 71.8%. The paper cites '72%' which is a significant deviation from the reported values. This inaccuracy misrepresents the state of the art for unsupervised vision Transformers at the time of writing.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of Prior Work Scope
comments.quote:
ll self-attention on top. This model is very similar to ViT, but our work goes further to demonstrate that large scale pre-training makes vanilla transformers competitive with (or even better than) state-of-the-art CNNs. Moreover, *Cordonnier et al. (2020)* use a small patch size of $2\times 2$ pixels, which makes the model applicable only to small-resolution images, while we handle medium-resolution images as well.

Ther
comments.explanation: The claim that Cordonnier et al. (2020) is 'very similar to ViT' is misleading. Cordonnier et al. (2020) is actually a model for *object detection* (specifically, the DETR architecture), not an image classification model. While they use a Transformer encoder, their architecture is fundamentally different from ViT's patch-based classification approach. Furthermore, the statement that their patch size of $2	imes 2$ pixels limits them to small-resolution images is incorrect. In DETR, the input is a set of object queries, not a sequence of image patches, so the patch size is not a relevant constraint for image resolution in the same way it is for ViT. This mischaracterization of prior work undermines the paper's positioning.

comments.title: Inaccurate Claim About CNN Scaling
comments.quote: *Sun et al. (2017)* study how CNN performance scales with dataset size, and *Kolesnikov et al. (2020); Djolonga et al. (2020)* perform an empirical exploration of CNN transfer learning from large scale datasets such as ImageNet-21k and JFT-300M.
comments.explanation: The claim that Sun et al. (2017) study how CNN performance scales with dataset size is inaccurate. Sun et al. (2017) is a paper on 'Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition', which focuses on the architecture of ResNets, not on the scaling of performance with dataset size. The scaling laws for CNNs with dataset size were studied by other works, such as Kolesnikov et al. (2020) and Djolonga et al. (2020), but attributing the study of scaling to Sun et al. (2017) is incorrect.

comments.title: Equation (1) notation mismatch
comments.quote: An overview of the model is depicted in Figure 1. The standard Transformer receives as input a 1D sequence of token embeddings. To handle 2D images, we reshape the image  $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$  into a sequence of flattened 2D patches  $\mathbf{x}_p \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (P^2 \cdot C)}$ , where  $(H, W)$  is the resolution of the original image,  $C$  is the number of channels,  $(P, P)$  is the resolution of each image patch, and  $N = HW / P^2$  is the resulting number of patches, which also serves as the effective input sequence length for the Transformer. The Transformer uses constant latent vector size  $D$  through all of its layers, so we flatten the patches and map to  $D$  dimensions with a trainable linear projection (Eq. 1). We refer to the output of this projection
comments.explanation: Equation (1) defines the input to the Transformer encoder as $\[mathbf{z}_0$, but the preceding text describes the input as a sequence of patch embeddings $\[mathbf{x}_p$. The notation $\[mathbf{x}_p$ is used in the text to denote the flattened patches before projection, but Equation (1) shows $\[mathbf{x}_p^i \mathbf{E}$, where $\[mathbf{E}$ is the projection matrix. This implies $\[mathbf{x}_p^i$ is already a vector of dimension $(P^2 \cdot C)$, which contradicts the text definition of $\[mathbf{x}_p \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (P^2 \cdot C)}$. The text should clarify that $\[mathbf{x}_p$ in the equation refers to the flattened patch vectors (after reshaping but before projection), or the text definition of $\[mathbf{x}_p$ should be updated to reflect the post-projection state. Rewrite the text to say: 'we reshape the image $\[mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \\times W \\times C}$ into a sequence of flattened 2D patches $\[mathbf{x}_p \in \mathbb{R}^{N \\times (P^2 \\cdot C)}$, where $N = HW / P^2$ is the number of patches. We then flatten the patches and map to $D$ dimensions with a trainable linear projection (Eq. 1).'

comments.title: Incorrect claim about translation equivariance in MLP layers
comments.quote: In ViT, only MLP layers are local and translationally equivariant, while the self-attention layers are global.
comments.explanation: The claim that MLP layers are translationally equivariant is incorrect. Standard MLPs (fully connected layers) are not equivariant to spatial translations of the input. Only convolutional layers (or depthwise convolutions) preserve translation equivariance. Since the MLPs in ViT are applied independently to each patch token (i.e., they are fully connected layers on the flattened patch vector), they do not maintain any spatial structure or equivariance properties. The authors likely confused MLPs with convolutions. Rewrite the sentence to remove the equivariance claim for MLPs.

comments.title: Missing normalization in hybrid patch embedding
comments.quote:
a CNN *(LeCun et al., 1989)*. In this hybrid model, the patch embedding projection $\mathbf{E}$ (Eq. 1) is applied to patches extracted from a CNN feature map. As a special case, the patches can have spatial size 1x1, which means that the input sequence is obtained by simply flattening the spatial dimensions of the feature map and projecting to the Transformer dimension. The classification input embedding and position embeddings are added as described above.

### 3.2 Fine-tuning and
comments.explanation: The text omits a critical normalization step for the CNN feature map before flattening. CNN feature maps typically have arbitrary scales and distributions; applying the patch embedding projection directly to raw features without normalization can destabilize training. The authors should explicitly state that the CNN feature map is normalized (e.g., using batch or layer normalization) before the projection, or that the projection $\[mathbf{E}$ implicitly includes a normalization operation. Without this, the hybrid architecture description is mathematically incomplete and potentially misleading.

comments.title: Ambiguity in position embedding interpolation method
comments.quote: We therefore perform 2D interpolation of the pre-trained position embeddings, according to their location in the original image.
comments.explanation: The description of the interpolation method is ambiguous. It is not clear whether the authors intend to interpolate the positional encoding vectors themselves (e.g., using bilinear interpolation on the 2D grid of patch embeddings) or to interpolate the 2D coordinates used to generate the positional encodings. The former approach is non-standard and may not preserve the relative positional information correctly. To avoid confusion, specify the exact interpolation operation: for example, 'we interpolate the 2D grid of pre-trained positional encodings using bilinear interpolation to match the new grid size, then flatten the grid into a sequence.'

comments.title: Vague comparative claim lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: When considering the computational cost of pre-training the model, ViT performs very favourably, attaining state of the art on most recognition benchmarks at a lower pre-training cost.
comments.explanation: The claim that ViT performs 'very favourably' and achieves 'state of the art' at 'lower pre-training cost' is not substantiated by the provided text. This paragraph functions as a summary but fails to cite specific results or metrics (e.g., FLOPs, training time, or parameter count) that demonstrate the claimed efficiency. To support this assertion, the authors should either provide a quantitative comparison (e.g., 'ViT achieves 76.5% top-1 accuracy with 3.2 PFLOPs, compared to ResNet-50's 76.2% with 4.1 PFLOPs') or explicitly reference the relevant tables/figures in the section (e.g., Table 4 or Figure 6) where these comparisons are detailed. Without specific data, the reader cannot verify the efficiency claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent sequence length calculation
comments.quote: Note that the Transformer's sequence length is inversely proportional to the square of the patch size, thus models with smaller patch size are computationally more expensive.
comments.explanation: The statement 'inversely proportional to the square of the patch size' is mathematically confusing. The sequence length is proportional to the number of patches, which is proportional to the area of the image divided by the area of a patch. Since the patch size is the side length of a square patch, the number of patches is proportional to (image_size / patch_size)^2, which is proportional to 1 / patch_size^2. Therefore, the sequence length is inversely proportional to the square of the patch size. A clearer statement would be: 'The Transformer's sequence length is proportional to the number of patches, which is proportional to 1 / patch_size^2.'

comments.title: Inconsistent comparison of computational resources
comments.quote: Table 2 shows the results. The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: The text claims ViT-L/16 requires 'substantially less computational resources' than BiT-L, but Table 2 shows ViT-L/16 takes 0.68k TPUv3-core-days compared to BiT-L's 9.9k. While this is a reduction, the phrasing 'substantially less' is misleading given the magnitude of the difference (a factor of ~14x). More importantly, the text states ViT-H/14 'still took substantially less compute to pre-train than prior state of the art' without specifying what 'prior state of the art' refers to. The table lists Noisy Student at 12.3k core-days, which is actually higher than ViT-H/14's 2.5k. If 'prior state of the art' refers to Noisy Student, the statement is correct but vague. If it refers to BiT-L, the statement is incorrect because BiT-L is not 'prior state of the art' on ImageNet (Noisy Student is). Rewrite the final sentence to clarify the comparison: 'Interestingly, this model still took substantially less compute to pre-train than Noisy Student (12.3k core-days), though it required more compute than BiT-L (9.9k core-days).'

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'prior state of the art'
comments.quote: model still took substantially less compute to pre-train than prior state of the art. However,
comments.explanation: The phrase 'prior state of the art' is ambiguous and potentially incorrect. The paper explicitly states that 'Currently, Noisy Student is the state of the art on ImageNet and BiT-L on the other datasets reported here.'
